scripture.text: God is our refuge and strength, a very present help in trouble. Therefore will we not fear, though the earth do change, and though the mountains be shaken into the heart of the seas. ... Be still, and know that I am God. ... Jehovah of hosts is with us; the God of Jacob is our refuge.
Come unto me, all ye that labor and are heavy laden, and I will give you rest. Take my yoke upon you, and learn of me; for I am meek and lowly in heart: and ye shall find rest unto your souls.
It is of Jehovah's lovingkindnesses that we are not consumed, because his compassions fail not. They are new every morning; great is thy faithfulness.

O Jehovah, thou hast searched me, and known me. Thou knowest my downsitting and mine uprising; Thou understandest my thought afar off. Thou searchest out my path and my lying down, And art acquainted with all my ways. For there is not a word in my tongue, But, lo, O Jehovah, thou knowest it altogether.
but God, being rich in mercy, for his great love wherewith he loved us, even when we were dead through our trespasses, made us alive together with Christ (by grace have ye been saved),
(for we walk by faith, not by sight);

Let not your heart be troubled: believe in God, believe also in me. In my Father's house are many mansions; if it were not so, I would have told you; for I go to prepare a place for you. And if I go and prepare a place for you, I come again, and will receive you unto myself; that where I am, there ye may be also.

Enter into his gates with thanksgiving, and into his courts with praise; give thanks unto him, and bless his name. For Jehovah is good; his lovingkindness endureth for ever, and his faithfulness unto all generations.

segments.v:
Neil was amazed. If they had rushed ahead with their own program months earlier, they would have missed this opportunity to partner with other churches and have a much greater impact.

“How did you know to wait?” Neil asked.

Pastor Moonsamy explained, “I’ve learned that God’s timing is always perfect, even when it doesn’t make sense to us. When we wait for His signal, we receive His best plans. Scripture doesn’t say those who hurry will renew their strength. It says those who wait on the Lord will renew their strength.”

What amazed Elena most was how Bella could rest no matter what was happening around her. A garbage truck rumbled down the street, and Bella barely lifted her head. The neighbor’s lawnmower started up, but Bella stayed peaceful. A squirrel ran across the fence just feet from her nose, but Bella simply opened one eye, looked, and went back to resting.

Elena realized that Bella somehow knew she was safe. The yard was secure. Her food bowl was full. Elena was watching over her. That was enough for Bella to rest completely.

Elena remembered Jesus’s words: “Come to me and I will give you rest.” Not “work harder for me” or “prove your worth to me.” Come and rest.

She thought about how Jesus himself had rested. He slept in a boat during a storm while His disciples worried and panicked. That wasn’t because Jesus didn’t care about the danger. It was because He trusted His Father completely.

Elena realized she had been carrying burdens that weren’t meant for her shoulders. She worried about every administrative detail as if the church would fall apart without her constant attention. She stressed about events as if their success was her responsibility alone. She had forgotten that the church belonged to God, not to her.

Mrs. Rodriguez nodded knowingly. “Ah, yes. Sometimes we can learn from our furry friends, can’t we? They don’t worry about tomorrow’s problems like we do.”

From that week forward, Elena began to approach her church work differently. She still served faithfully and worked hard, but she also built regular times of rest into her schedule. She learned to trust that God could handle the church’s needs even when she took breaks.

And every time Elena felt the old anxiety creeping back, she remembered Bella sleeping peacefully in the sunshine, completely trusting that everything important was taken care of. If a dog could rest in such trust, surely she could learn to rest in God’s care too.

Over the following weeks, Pastor Martinez found himself thinking about Gida’s faithful waiting. The dog had no control over when David would return. He couldn’t call the military for updates or check deployment schedules online. All Gida could do was wait with hope and trust.

Pastor Martinez began to see similarities between Gida’s situation and his own prayers about the church building. He had been treating his waiting like a problem to solve rather than an opportunity to trust. He had grown impatient and discouraged, assuming that delayed answers meant his requests had been denied.

When visitors come to the house, other neighborhood dogs might race to the fence, barking to let everyone know and wanting attention. Mola looks from her spot in the sun, and wags her tail, just a bit to say, “I see you.” She seems to understand something that many others don’t: she already has a place, a name, and a family. She doesn’t need to earn what she already has.

This behavior becomes even more interesting during stressful situations. When construction noise fills the air, or when storms bring thunder and lightning, Mola doesn’t panic or hide. She simply moves closer to her family members. Her calm presence suggests a deep security, not in her circumstances, but in her relationships.

Jesus didn’t promise His followers that following Him would always be exciting or that every day would bring big victories. What He promised was His presence: “I am with you always, even to the end of the world.” That’s the heart of faithfulness.

Good dogs know how to sit. They don’t rush things. They don’t panic; well most don’t. They just wait. And when they’re told to sit, they usually do. Not always right away. Sometimes they squirm a little. But in the end, they stay.

Oddly enough, that, for a lot of us human beings, is really hard to do. When life gets hard, we want to fix it. We want to do something about it, anything, to make it better. But maybe sometimes, what God wants from us is something quite different, much smaller.

He looked up, smiled, and answered, “Waiting on the Lord. He’s almost never early. But He’s never late either.”

He wasn’t doing nothing. He was waiting, with hope. He was still, but not because he had given up. Because he had hope and believed help was coming.

The Bible says, “Be still, and know that I am God.” That doesn’t mean we stop caring. It means we stop trying to be in control.

That verse isn’t just for people who live quiet lives. It’s for people like us. The ones who are tired. The ones who are hurting. The ones who’ve tried everything, and now… maybe it’s time to stop and sit for a while.

We don’t know what that looks like for you. But we are learning that being still doesn’t mean we have failed. It just means we are learning to trust. Trust in the Lord. Trust that the Lord will take care of us.

And maybe, when life feels too hard, trusting the Lord and being still is enough for today.

We are sure there have been times in our lives when we have ran ahead of God. We have hastedly made decisions before we prayed. We have tried to fix things on our own. We tell ourselves we were being “proactive,” but really, we were impatient and maybe a little scared to wait. We lacked patience. We lacked trust.

And then there were other times when we stopped walking altogether. We got stuck. Completely lost. We didn’t know where to go, so we just sat down, like a dog that refuses to move in the middle of a sidewalk.

Faith is like that. It’s not about having our own map, figuring out our destination, and then coming up with a plan to get there. Sure, we need to do our part, but faith is about knowing and trusting the One who’s leading. And us following.

There was a woman who was going through something really difficult. She was homeless and sick, but she still came to church on Sundays. She said she felt safer there, even if it was just for an hour.

Don’t we want to live like that? To walk like that. Close to the Lord. Quietly. Trusting in the Lord. Faithfully. Not trying to rush off somewhere. Not running away. Just following the Lord. Right next to the Lord.

Because honestly, we don’t need to know everything about the road ahead. We just need to know the Lord knows the road ahead and that we are not walking alone.

That the dogs trust their owners is admirable. Because we don’t always trust like that. We worry God might have forgotten about us. Might not take care of our needs. So we make backup plans. We check our wallets even when we just checked it five minutes ago. We worry.

We want to believe God will take care of us, but sometimes it’s hard. Especially when things don’t go the way we think they should. When things run low. Our checking accounts. Food in the fridge. Time. Energy. Hope. Especially when I’ve been waiting a while.

One morning, he was asked how he stayed so calm. He said, “I’ve gone to bed hungry plenty of times. But God hasn’t forgotten me.”

That is trust.

In the Bible, Jesus tells His followers not to worry about food or clothes. He says the birds don’t store up food, and the flowers don’t work for beauty. But God feeds and clothes them anyway. Then He says, “Aren’t you worth more than they are?” God tells us we are.

It’s not always easy to believe that. But don’t we believe what Jesus is saying is true? God gives us what we need. Maybe not everything we ask for, but enough to keep going. Not as soon as possible, but in God’s perfect timing. He gives us enough to remind us we’re not forgotten. And sometimes much more than enough.

Dogs trust their person to care for them. They sit near the bowl. They wait. Sometimes patiently and for longer than they would like. They don’t understand where the food comes from. But they know who gives it. Who fills up their bowl with good food.

Isn’t that the kind of trusting heart we want? A simple heart full of thanksgiving. A trusting heart that trusts the Hand that feeds us.

We are all learning. To worry less. To ask simply. To wait with hope. Because even when we don’t see it yet, we believe the bowl will be filled by our good Lord.

Dogs can sleep almost anywhere.

Haven’t we seen them stretched out on sidewalks? Curled up in noisy shelters. Tucked under tables during thunderstorms. Sometimes they sleep with one eye open, but still they sleep.

And I’ve wondered how they do that. How can they rest in places that feel so uncertain?

Part of the answer is simple: they trust who they’re with. They know someone is watching over them. And that’s enough. More than enough for them to sleep in peace.

There are dogs that sometimes sleep at our feet. Whenever we move, they lift their heads. Just a little, to see if we were still there. Then they would sigh and settle back down. As long as we were near, they could sleep.

Haven’t there been been plenty of nights in our lives when we just couldn’t fall sleep? It might have been stress. Fear. Pain. Pain in our hearts. Pain in our bodies. Thoughts that run wild after the lights go out. The body might have been tired, but the mind kept spinning. It’s hard to rest when the world feels like it’s falling apart. Or when our own lives feel too uncertain.

But in the Psalms, there’s a verse that says, “I will lie down and sleep in peace, for You alone, Lord, make me dwell in safety.” It doesn’t say the Lord made the world a peaceful place. It doesn’t say the problems went away. It just says: the Lord is with us. We are safe. We can rest now.

Dogs understand this in their own way. They don’t control much. They don’t know what tomorrow holds. But when they know they’re cared for, they are able to sleep. Not because their lives are perfect. But because they believe they’re protected and taken care of by their person.

Rest is not just something the body needs. It’s something the heart needs, too. And sometimes, the bravest thing we can do at the end of a hard day is to close our eyes and trust that God will take care of whatever we are worrying about.

So, let’s stop carrying everything and put our worries down, at the foot of our Lord. Let’s stop rehearsing all the what-ifs. Instead, let’s breathe. Physically and spiritually. And be still. And sleep, like someone who is safely loved. Because we are loved.

There’s so much in the world pulling for our attention: screens, headlines, pressure to do more, have more, be more, and more screens. But peace comes when we follow the One who gives real peace.

Slow down. Look at what we are chasing after. If it isn’t something that will give us what really matters, stop. And then run after God and His kingdom.

We argue. We worry. We pick sides. And before long, we’ve forgotten what we were supposed to be doing in the first place.

The Bible says, “Set your minds on things above, not on earthly things.” (Colossians 3:2) That’s hard to do when you’re sprinting after every distraction. But staying focused doesn’t mean you don’t care. It means you care enough not to waste your energy on noise.

Dogs chase squirrels because it’s instinct. People chase them because it feels like action; we feel like we’re doing something.

03-one-voice-one-leash-one-lord-020.book_title: One Voice, One Leash, One Lord

segments.v:
But real wisdom isn’t about reacting to everything. It’s about knowing what matters — and letting the rest go.

Jesus wasn’t easily distracted. When people tried to trap Him with clever questions or emotional arguments, He didn’t take the bait. He saw right through the noise and spoke to the heart of the issue. Every time.

You don’t have to respond to everything. You don’t have to fix every argument. And you definitely don’t have to chase every squirrel.

Some things are better left in the tree.

03-one-voice-one-leash-one-lord-021.book_title: One Voice, One Leash, One Lord

segments.v:
But choices made out of fear are almost always bad choices. It rushes. It exaggerates. It forgets that God is still in charge. He has always been and will always be.

Jesus once told His followers to stay awake — to be ready. But He never told them to panic. In fact, again and again, He said, “Do not be afraid.”

We were never meant to live with our hearts clenched. Being faithful doesn’t mean being frightened. Quite the opposite: it means keeping your eyes open and your feet steady.

03-one-voice-one-leash-one-lord-026.book_title: One Voice, One Leash, One Lord

segments.v:
Like a good dog watching the gate — not pacing, not growling, just… watching.

Trust that when it’s time to act, the Master will let you know. And until then, you can sit quietly. Because you’re not guarding the world on your own.

You’re just doing your part. And that’s enough.

03-one-voice-one-leash-one-lord-027.book_title: One Voice, One Leash, One Lord

segments.v:
You don’t need to see everything to stay faithful. Often it’s just the opposite: our faith grows when we don’t see much at all. You just need to know Who you’re walking with. Or Who is walking with us.

Sometimes that means standing still. Sometimes that means taking one small, careful step. But always, it means listening for His voice.

The world will try to rush you. Push you. Confuse you. But you don’t have to move until the Shepherd puts out His hand for yours and says, “Come.”

03-one-voice-one-leash-one-lord-042.book_title: One Voice, One Leash, One Lord

segments.v:
So wait in the fog if you have to. Not with fear, but with faith. Because He hasn’t forgotten you. And He’s never late.

Final Thoughts

Politics can make us feel like we always have to pick a side, raise our voice, or brace for a fight. But maybe that’s not the only way.

Maybe we don’t have to match the noise. Maybe we don’t have to chase every issue, prove every point, or win every argument. Maybe what the world really needs — what God is asking of us — is something simpler.

03-one-voice-one-leash-one-lord-043.book_title: One Voice, One Leash, One Lord

segments.v:
They guard what matters. They don’t give up when things get tough. And they’re don’t distracted by the crowd. Well, the good ones anyway.

That’s what we’re called to be — faithful. Not loud, but quiet and steady. Not driven by fear, but filled by His love and led by the One who sees clearly, even when we can’t.

So if you ever feel overwhelmed by the state of the world, take a breath. Look up. Remember who you belong to. You don’t have to carry it all. You can’t, even if you tried. But you can be faithful with what’s right in front of you.

03-one-voice-one-leash-one-lord-045.book_title: One Voice, One Leash, One Lord

segments.v:
Even leaving was about patience. About reading the moment. About moving only when movement made sense.

The director thought about all the deals he’d rushed into. All the opportunities he’d chased because everyone else was chasing them. All the doors he’d tried to force open.

Maybe the owner and his old dog understood something he was just beginning to learn: True strength isn’t in the speed of your response. It’s in knowing when response is actually needed.

Sometimes the best business strategy looks like an old dog by his owner’s desk: calm, watchful, ready to move when the moment is right, and completely still until then.

In business, as in faith, patience isn’t passive. It’s active trust. It’s watching carefully while others panic. It’s being ready while others rush. It’s knowing that the One who controls all outcomes has perfect timing.

The dog had learned it instinctively. Now the director was beginning to understand: Sometimes the most powerful move is to wait.

“Wisdom isn’t knowing when to run,” he told his son, who was learning the business. “It’s knowing when to wait and when waiting turns into the right time to move.”

Pepper was teaching the same lesson to a young stray dog that had shown up at the farm. The younger dog wanted to chase everything immediately. Pepper would hold him back with his body, making him wait, showing him the difference between frantic activity and purposeful action.

Scripture says there’s a time for everything. A time to plant and a time to harvest. A time to wait and a time to run.

Sky was a Golden Retriever who lived with an elderly woman in a small cottage. Every morning at six, Sky would sit by his bowl in the kitchen and wait. He never paced. He never whined. He never searched the floor for crumbs or tried to open the pantry door.

He simply sat and waited.

The woman’s routine varied each day. Sometimes she fed him at six-fifteen. Sometimes not until seven. On difficult mornings when her arthritis flared up, it might be even later. But Sky never panicked. He waited with quiet confidence because he knew she would feed him. She always had.

On the day they ran out of food, the woman sat at her kitchen table and cried. Sky walked over and rested his head on her knee. She looked down at him, this dog who had no idea about pension checks or bank accounts or bills, and yet somehow remained peaceful.

That afternoon, a neighbor knocked on the door. She had bought too much dog food by mistake, she said. Would Sky like some? The woman accepted it gratefully, tears filling her eyes again.

Sky knew better. He understood what she was still learning.

Scripture says it plainly: “Give us this day our daily bread.”

Give us this day our daily bread: today’s provision, not tomorrow’s worry. Because the One who feeds the birds and clothes the flowers has never once forgotten His own.

Sky sat by his bowl every morning for years. Some mornings the food came early. Some mornings it came late. But it always came.

Because provision doesn’t come from the paycheck or the pension or the deal we’re counting on. It comes from the Provider who has never missed a single day.

Maybe it’s the notification ping that pulls us into a social media rabbit hole right when we meant to start homework. Maybe it’s the friend drama that consumes our thoughts during the class we actually need to pay attention to. Maybe it’s the worry about college applications that keeps us from being present with the people right in front of us.

The world is full of fire hydrants and rustling bushes, metaphorically speaking. There will always be something seemingly urgent demanding our immediate attention. But just like Rosie’s owner gently guides her back to the path, we need something to keep us focused on what actually matters.
Let us run with perseverance the race marked out for us, fixing our eyes on Jesus, the pioneer and perfecter of faith.

The Christian life isn’t a leisurely stroll where we can investigate every interesting distraction. It’s a race with a specific course and a clear finish line. But notice what keeps us on track: fixing our eyes on Jesus.

When we keep looking at Him, His character, His love, His purposes, the distractions lose their pull. The social media drama seems less important when we remember we’re called to love others well. The academic pressure feels less overwhelming when we remember our identity comes from being God’s children, not our GPA.

This doesn’t mean we ignore everything else or become boring people with no interests. It means we learn to distinguish between what deserves our attention and what’s just noise. It means developing the discipline to stay focused on our actual direction instead of chasing every shiny thing that crosses our path.

Just like Rosie’s owner uses gentle tugs to redirect her, set up gentle reminders to redirect ourselves. A Bible verse as our phone wallpaper. Asking ourselves, “Is this helping me love God and others better?” before making decisions. Having a friend who can lovingly call us out when we’re chasing squirrels.

The funny thing is, Luna is never in danger of running out of treats. Her humans love her and provide for her daily. But she can’t seem to believe that tomorrow’s treats are as guaranteed as today’s. So she hoards, and hoards, and hoards. All while missing out on the simple joy of actually enjoying what she has right now.

Luna’s treat hoarding might seem silly, but it reveals something deep about human nature, too. We’re all prone to thinking there’s not enough to go around. So we’d better grab what we can and hold on tight.

For teenagers, this might show up in different ways. Hoarding compliments. Being afraid to encourage others because it might diminish our own worth. Hoarding opportunities, unwilling to help a classmate succeed because we think their success somehow threatens ours. Hoarding time, energy, or attention, convinced that if we give too much away, we’ll run out.

But here’s what Luna misses and what we often miss too: hoarding doesn’t actually create security. It creates anxiety. Those treats hidden under the couch aren’t bringing Luna joy; they’re just making her constantly worry about protecting them. Meanwhile, she’s missing out on the pleasure of actually experiencing generosity, both giving and receiving.
Give, and it will be given to you. A good measure, pressed down, shaken together and running over, will be poured into your lap. For with the measure you use, it will be measured to you.

This isn’t a magic formula for getting rich quick. It’s a spiritual truth about how God’s kingdom works. When we live with open hands instead of clenched fists, we discover that God’s supply never runs out. There’s always enough love, enough grace, enough joy to share.

Jesus demonstrated this perfectly with the feeding of the five thousand. The disciples saw a problem: not enough food for way too many people. But Jesus saw an opportunity: when we give what we have to God, He multiplies it beyond what we could imagine.

This means we can be generous with our encouragement, our time, our talents, our friendship, and even with all the stuff we have. We can celebrate others’ successes without feeling threatened. We can share our lunch with someone who forgot theirs. We

Notice what happens. Not just to others, but to us. We’ll probably find that giving actually creates more joy than hoarding ever could. We might discover that there’s always enough kindness to go around. We might even realize that the more we give away, the more we seem to have.

Luna will probably keep hiding treats under the couch cushions. But we don’t have to live like there’s not enough good things coming. God’s supply never runs out.

Like Pepper, we can develop protective strategies that make perfect sense given our experiences. We might become suspicious of new people. We might hold back our true feelings to avoid vulnerability. We might assume the worst about others’ motives. We might try to control everything to prevent future hurt.

The problem is, while these strategies protect us from potential pain, they also protect us from potential joy. When we’re so focused on avoiding hurt that we can’t trust anyone, we miss out on the deep connections God designed us to experience.
Trust in the Lord with all your heart and lean not on your own understanding; in all your ways submit to him, and he will make your paths straight.

This doesn’t mean we should be naive or ignore red flags in relationships. God gave us wisdom and discernment for good reasons. But it does mean that our ultimate trust should be placed in Someone who will never let us down, never betray us, never use our vulnerability against us.

The irony is that our culture’s obsession with constant activity often makes us less effective, not more. When we’re exhausted, we make poor decisions. When we’re stressed, we can’t think clearly. When we’re running on empty, we’re more likely to snap at people we care about.

“Come to me, all you who are weary and burdened, and I will give you rest. Take my yoke upon you and learn from me, for I am gentle and humble in heart, and you will find rest for your souls.”

But here’s the thing about Bear’s courage: it’s not careless. He doesn’t pick fights or look for trouble.

Most of the time, he’s perfectly content to let the mail carrier, delivery drivers, and friendly neighbors go about their business. He’s not afraid of everything, and he’s not aggressive toward everyone. His courage is purposeful: showing courage only when someone he loves needs protection.

Bear’s example shows us what real courage looks like. It’s not the absence of fear; it’s acting rightly even when he has fear, when something important is at stake. It’s not about being aggressive or confrontational. It’s about standing up when standing up matters.
Have I not commanded you? Be strong and courageous. Do not be afraid; do not be discouraged, for the Lord your God will be with you wherever you go.

God spoke these words to Joshua when he was about to lead the Israelites into unknown territory. Joshua was probably terrified: he had big shoes to fill and huge challenges ahead. But God didn’t say, “Don’t be afraid because nothing bad will happen.” He said, “Don’t be afraid because I’ll be with you.”

That’s the secret to Bear’s courage and the secret to ours: we don’t have to be strong enough to handle everything alone. We just have to be willing to act on what’s right, trusting that God will provide what we need in the moment.

Courage isn’t about being fearless. It’s about being faithful despite our fears. Bear was probably nervous when he confronted that stranger, but Emma’s safety mattered more than his comfort. Similarly, when we choose courage, we’re saying that something matters more than our own comfort or safety.

He doesn’t pace anxiously or give up after ten minutes. He doesn’t need to be reminded all the time that the bus really is on its way. He just waits with calm certainty, because in his experience, Katie always comes home.

Max’s window waiting is a perfect picture of hope. Not the wishful-thinking kind of hope (like hoping it won’t rain on our outdoor party), but the kind of hope that trusts without doubting. Max waits because he knows Katie’s character, he knows her promises, and he’s experienced her faithfulness day after day.

Teenagers require a lot of hope. Hope that hard work in school will pay off. Hope that the friendships will last. Hope that dreams are worth the effort. Hope that the world can be better.

But sometimes hope feels risky. What if we trust someone and they let us down? What if we work toward something and it doesn’t happen? What if we hope for good things, but life doesn’t go the way we wanted? It can seem safer to expect less, hope for less, trust less.
And hope does not put us to shame, because God’s love has been poured out into our hearts through the Holy Spirit, who has been given to us.

This verse promises that hope in God never leads to disappointment. Not because everything always goes the way we want, but because God’s love is the one thing that never fails. Katie might one day not go to school for one reason or another, but God’s character never changes. His promises never expire. His love never runs out.

This means we can wait like Max does with trust in God’s goodness, even when we can’t see it yet. We can trust Him to guide us when we make decisions, His strength in our struggles, His peace in our anxiety, and His purpose in our lives. Not because we’re optimistic people, but because we know His track record.

Biblical hope isn’t naive or passive. It’s active trust based on God’s proven faithfulness. It says, “I don't know exactly how this will work out, but I know who’s in control, and I trust His heart toward me.”

This kind of hope changes how we live right now. We can work hard in school without being anxious about the future, because we trust God with the outcomes. We can invest in relationships without fear of being rejected, because our security comes from God’s unchanging love. We can take healthy risks and try new things, because we know that even failures can be part of God’s good plan for our growth. We can face difficult times with courage, because we trust that God can bring good from even painful situations.

When our life implodes from a major mistake, our perspective becomes very narrow. We see the immediate consequences: the disappointed faces, the closed doors, the opportunities that disappear. We can’t see the conversations happening behind the scenes, the people working on our behalf, the possibilities being created outside our limited view.

We assume that if we can’t see progress being made, no progress is being made. We give up hope because our small window doesn’t show us the whole picture.

‘For My thoughts are not your thoughts, neither are your ways My ways,’ declares the Lord. ‘As the heavens are higher than the earth, so are My ways higher than your ways and My thoughts than your thoughts.’

God spoke these words to people who felt abandoned and hopeless. From their limited perspective, it looked like God had forgotten them, like their situation would never improve, like they were stuck forever in the consequences of past mistakes.

But God was working in ways and places they couldn’t see. His thoughts were operating on a completely different level than their thoughts. His ways of solving problems were beyond their ability to imagine or perceive.

The distance between Earth and Heaven isn’t just physical; it’s a metaphor for how much bigger God’s perspective is than ours. We see our kennel view; He sees the whole yard and everything beyond it.

Ziggy’s anxiety and despair were based on incomplete information. He couldn’t see Maria’s office where

When we’re dealing with the aftermath of a major mistake, we enter a season of indefinite waiting. Waiting for trust to be rebuilt. Waiting for consequences to resolve. Waiting for opportunities to return. Waiting for forgiveness, from others and from ourselves.

The hardest part is that no one can give us a timeline. We can’t know when our spouse will be ready to work on the marriage again, when employers will be willing to hire us, when our family or friends will want a relationship with us again. We have to learn to wait well without knowing how long the waiting will last.
Wait for the Lord; be strong and take heart and wait for the Lord.

David wrote this during a period when his life was in danger and his future was uncertain. He didn’t know when his circumstances would change, but he knew that passive waiting would destroy him. So he chose active waiting: waiting with strength, waiting with courage, waiting with purpose.

The phrase “take heart” means to be firm, to be established, to find courage. David’s waiting wasn’t passive resignation; it was determined hope. He was going to wait, but he was going to wait like someone who believed good things were still possible.

“So what do I do?”

“You pray. You think. You be honest with yourself about why you’re drawn to this boy. Is it him, or is it escape from your mother’s expectations? And remember that even if you make the wrong choice, we have God’s mercy and His grace.”

“Mom would never forgive me if I dated him.”

“Your mother has forgiven worse in others. She counsels unwed mothers at the mission, former addicts, people who’ve made every mistake. She shows them grace daily. She just struggles to show it to you because she loves you too much to watch you struggle.”

She looked at Jax. The warrior dog was trembling under the furniture. “You know what my dad always says?” she asked him. “He has this Bible verse he quotes when things get tough. When working with the pilots isn’t going well, when the generals are breathing down his neck, when Mom cries in the bathroom. She thinks we can’t hear her.”

The thunder cracked again. Jax pressed himself farther back.

“’God is our refuge and strength,’” the daughter recited, “’an ever-present help in trouble.’ Psalm 46:1. He says it means God is our home. Not a place. Not a base. Not a house. God himself is where we live.”

“How did you do that?” the son whispered.

The daughter didn’t look up. She was afraid to break the moment. “I didn’t do anything. I just stayed. Sometimes that’s all anyone needs. Someone to stay when everything else keeps changing.”

The father thought of his friend, Jax’s handler. He remembered how she’d talked about the dog. “He knows my heart,” she’d said. Maybe that’s what they’d all forgotten. They were always moving. They were always adapting. They needed to feel known. Moving together wasn’t enough.

The verse the father had quoted so often took on new meaning. God as refuge didn’t mean God would stop the moves, stop the changes, stop the losses. It meant He was the constant presence through them all. He was the home that traveled with them.

Several months into their tour at Osan, Jax still perked up at certain sounds and scents. His ears would lift. His body would orient toward something only he detected. But he also sprawled across the daughter’s bed during homework. He brought his ball to the son for fetch. He leaned against the mother’s legs while she cooked. He walked beside the father with steady presence.

The father thought about those words. They needed to be still. They needed to stop worrying about the next move and where they would go next. Home wasn’t a place. Home was a presence.

Jax sighed deeply and relaxed.

The next morning at retreat, he still stiffened at the bugle’s first notes. His training would always be part of him. But now the family helped him through it. The daughter put her hand on his head, and the father spoke to him softly. Then the son tossed him a tennis ball. And when he brought it back, the mother gave him a treat.

The family would move again. Orders would come, boxes would be packed, goodbyes would be said. But they’d learned that home wasn’t about places. It was about God. He was their refuge and strength. He was with them wherever they went.

We met twelve dogs and twelve families, each facing a different version of the same struggle.

We saw the pressure to perform. We saw the expectation to follow the family path. We saw the loneliness of friendships that feel conditional and the exhaustion of hiding our struggles to protect the family name. We saw the anxiety that comes from moving again and again and the burden of being measured and evaluated. We saw the pain of feeling invisible and the responsibility of being the “good” one. We saw the guilt of being imperfect, the conflict of wanting something different, the challenge of finding rest, and the difficulty of letting go.

These struggles are real. They are not small. And they deserve to be named out loud.

If you are living under the pressure of your family’s expectations right now, whether those expectations come from cultural heritage, religious tradition, professional reputation, or dreams your parents have carried for you since before you were born, know this: the people who love you, even the ones putting pressure on you, usually do so because they want good things for you. They may not realize what that pressure is costing. They may not see the anxiety or the isolation or the exhaustion. They may not understand that the

The families we wrote about did not find easy answers. Real family dynamics are complicated. Cultural expectations do not disappear overnight. Generational patterns take time to change. But in each story, we saw families begin to change. We saw honest conversations begin. We saw new understanding develop. We saw grace extended and received.

That is what we pray for you. We do not pray that all pressure will disappear or that your family will suddenly understand everything. We pray for small steps and moments of honesty. We pray for the courage to name what you are feeling. We pray for people who see you as you really are. And above all, we pray for the deep assurance that your worth was never determined by your pedigree.

We’ve never met someone in ministry who didn’t need a good dog.

Some need one to walk with in the early mornings before the world gets demanding. Some need one to listen, really listen, without interrupting, correcting, or offering ministry advice. Some just need the reminder that joy doesn’t come from metrics, but from a wagging tail and someone who is glad that you came home.

Over the years, we’ve had many dogs. Some were loyal. Some were stubborn. One barked at shadows for reasons known only to God. But each one, in their own doggish way, taught us something we didn’t expect to learn: patience, presence, joy, grief, and what it means to be faithful when no one’s watching.

This isn’t a how-to book on church growth. This won’t fix your elders, your deacons, or your sound system. It’s not even a book of polished answers. It’s more like a box of dog biscuits: some soft, some crunchy, and a few that might need chewing on before the goodness comes through.

Sit. Stay. Preach. is about noticing. Noticing the things we often overlook in ministry and in life. It’s about finding God in the everyday moments. About knowing when to speak and when to stay silent. About remembering that even the Good Shepherd lets His sheep rest beside still waters. And maybe it’s okay if the pastor lies down too.

Mola had mastered something Min-jung had lost in all her busyness: the art of staying close.

Min-jung remembered Jesus’s words: “Remain in me, and I will remain in you.” Not “work for me” or “impress me with your programs.” Remain. Stay close. Be near.

She thought about Mary and Martha in the Bible. Martha had been busy serving Jesus, worried about getting everything perfect. But Mary had chosen to sit at Jesus’s feet, simply being with Him. And Jesus had gently told Martha that Mary had chosen the better way.

Min-jung realized she had been living like Martha for years. Always busy, always worried about the next event or meeting or problem to solve. She had been so focused on serving Jesus that she had forgotten to simply be with Him.

There’s a reason Scripture describes God as our shelter, our refuge, our hiding place. And there’s a reason Jesus calls us to remain in Him. We dash in and out of God’s presence like we’re picking up dry cleaning. We offer quick prayers between meetings and squeeze in hurried devotions before bed. But dwelling isn’t a drive-thru. It’s sitting down in the shade and staying there.

And somehow, in that quiet space, the lesson she had been wrestling with began to write itself. Not because she was working harder, but because she had finally stopped working and started trusting.

Mola had taught her something that church leaders often forget: sometimes the most spiritual thing we can do is sit down, stay close to God, and let His presence work in us. We don’t have to carry the weight of ministry on our shoulders. We don’t have to have all the answers or solve every problem. We just need to remain close to the One who does.

As she watched Bella sleep so peacefully, Elena began to think about her own relationship with rest. Elena realized she had been serving at the church for years, always busy, always working on something. She felt guilty whenever she wasn’t being productive, as if resting meant she wasn’t doing her part.

Watching Bella, Elena realized something important. Bella wasn’t resting because she was lazy or didn’t care. She was resting because she felt completely safe. Her food bowl was full, the yard was secure, and Elena was watching over her. That complete trust allowed Bella to rest peacefully. Elena understood that rest wasn’t laziness - it was trust.

They sat together for almost an hour. Elena felt her shoulders relax for the first time in weeks. Her mind slowly stopped racing through her mental to-do list. And in that quiet space, she remembered why she loved serving at the church in the first place. It wasn’t about the busy work or the endless tasks. It was about participating in God’s work and trusting Him with the results.

When Mrs. Rodriguez returned the following Sunday, she found Elena looking more rested than she had in months.

Elena discovered that when she rested well, she actually served better. She had more patience with difficult volunteers, more creativity for solving problems, and more joy in her daily tasks. Rest hadn’t made her less effective. It had made her more like the person God designed her to be.

During family parties when the house is filled with noise and activity, other dogs might get excited and run around trying to get attention from every visitor. Yuki says hello to the guests with a polite tail wag, then goes back to her usual spot. She’s learned that her job isn’t to entertain; it’s to be there for the one person who matters most to her.

As Yuki has grown older, her movements have slowed, but her faithfulness has never changed. Even when her joints are stiff, she still makes the trip from her bed to the living room each evening. Even when she’s tired, she finds the energy to be there for him. Her commitment to showing up has nothing to do with how she feels and everything to do with who she loves.

For church leaders who often feel overwhelmed by the pressure to do more, be more, and accomplish more, Yuki’s story offers a simple way to be faithful. Sometimes the most important ministry isn’t preaching the perfect sermon or starting the newest program or solving everyone’s problems. Sometimes it’s simply showing up every week and being with God and with His people.

Almost all church leaders feel like they’re not doing enough. But sometimes the most powerful ministry does not happen in the spotlight; it happens in quiet moments of being there for God’s people. Showing up for the same people week after week. Being available when someone needs prayer. Staying close to God especially when the work feels ordinary.

The world needs people who are faithful, who are there, and shows up day after day, week after week. The world needs people who understand that sometimes the greatest gift we can offer is simply being there, ready and available, at the feet of the One who loves us most. When we learn this kind of faithfulness, we discover that we don’t have to be impressive to be important. We just have to show up.

God sees every quiet act of showing up, and every decision to stay even when it would be easier to leave. He knows the importance of simply being there, and He’s honored by the kind of unshakeable faithfulness that keeps going.

Does today feel ordinary? Does ministry seem less than exciting? Does no one notice the work being done? Then remember Yuki. Sometimes the most important thing we can do is find our place at God’s feet and stay there. And be ready to follow wherever He leads us.

The Bible tells us about someone who understood this kind of faithfulness. Even when His disciples fell asleep during His darkest hour, Jesus stayed awake and kept praying. Even when the crowds turned against Him, He kept showing up. Even when following God’s plan led to suffering and death, He stayed exactly where He was supposed to be.

We didn’t write this book because we had all the answers.

Honestly, we wrote it because we needed to remember some things we’d forgotten. About God. About grace. About how following Jesus isn’t about being the best. Following Jesus is about staying close to Him.

All the dogs in our stories were far from perfect. Mola wasn’t a perfect dog, either. She didn’t know much. Or maybe she did and just didn’t let on. But she, along with all the others, taught us more than we expected: about loyalty, about resting, about how presence is sometimes the most powerful kind of ministry.

These stories came from watching dogs. They came from diverse neighborhoods where different languages are spoken, different foods are shared, but where the same God is loved and served.

We learned that faithfulness has no accent. Love speaks every language. And God’s grace translates perfectly into every culture.

We’re not theologians. We’re not pastors. We’re just two people — one deacon and one lay person — who love Jesus and have learned from good dogs in a beautifully diverse world. And if you made it all the way here, maybe you’re someone who’s tired, or hurting, or trying your best to be faithful in a world that often forgets what that word even means.

If that’s you, we hope these stories reminded you that God sees you. He hasn’t forgotten. You’re not too small. You’re not too late. You’re not too far gone.

You’re loved. So wherever you are, however you’re walking, and whatever language you pray in, we’ll just say this:

Keep going. Stay close to the Shepherd. And when in doubt: sit, stay, and listen for His voice.

He speaks to every heart. In every tongue. And sometimes, even through a good dog He places in our path now and then.

And the lessons started lining up. Strangely and beautifully, with the very truths we’d been reading in God’s Word.

Jin Kim is walking through stage 4 colon cancer. A season when life slows down whether you want it to or not. When the world turns a little slower, unimportant things go out of focus and certain important things come into focus. You start to notice what really matters. And often, it’s presence: the presence of God. God’s love. God’s grace. Everywhere, especially in unexpected places.

Dogs seem to know some things instinctively. Things God, in His mercy and grace, keeps showing us through them. We’re just two ordinary believers. One deacon. One layperson. Trying to love God well. Trying to walk faithfully. Still learning what it means to stay close to the Lord. And if you are too, this book is for you. He is with you. Always.

Life can be challenging. Life can hurt. Not all the time, maybe, but often enough. Things go wrong. Bodies break down. Plans fall apart. People let us down. And yet, somehow, we’re still here.

And if we’re still here, maybe there’s a way to show a little joy. A little hope. Even if it’s just a small wag.

Many of us have experienced or are currently experiencing challenging situations. We are living in shelters. Or are at hospitals recovering. Some of us are living in tents or in our cars. Yet, we can still smile.

Not because everything is fine. But because we can decide not to let the pain take away everything good. We can decide that we will continue to be joyful. Be kind. Be joyful. To laugh.

That doesn’t mean we pretend everything’s okay when it’s not. It doesn’t mean we fake a smile or hide how we feel. It just means… even here, even now, we can still be thankful. For something.

Like a warm cup of coffee. A friend who visits us at the hospital. A place to sit, rest, and just breathe. A word from Scripture that reminds us that we are still loved.

Dogs wag when they’re happy, yes. But sometimes they wag when they’re hurting, too. They wag because they know that the person who is near them cares for them. Because they trust the one who is with them.

That’s the kind of joy. Not a loud kind of joy. And it does not have to be perfect, at first. But it does need to be real.

The Bible says, “Rejoice in the Lord always.” Impossible? Think of it as more like wagging. Even in the middle of all the mess, all the pain, we can be glad that the Lord is here with us through it all.

Let’s learn. To look for the small things, the things that we take for granted that are still good. Like the sun on our face. The air we breathe. The fact that we can actually breathe. That we have an appetite. Are able to taste good food and say, “Oh, that’s good.” Joy can live alongside pain. Even on the hardest days. Go ahead and wag anyway. Because the Lord is with us.

We live in a world that forgets the weak. But dogs remind us and Jesus shows us that grace is never just for the strong. Grace is all the more for the weak, those who are hurting. They are the ones who appreciate grace down to their aching hearts. Let’s slow down. Notice the invisibles. Guard the weak, the way the Lord guards us.

Ever wonder what that is for us?

Some days, we seek comfort. Other days, it’s control. Sometimes we go after people’s approval. Hoping they’ll like us or notice what we’ve done. And there are days we don’t even know what we want. We just feel tired from all the running after things.

But deep down, we all want something real. Something that lasts. Something that’s worth the effort.

Jesus said, “Seek first the kingdom of God and His righteousness, and all these things will be added to you.”

And when they arrive, they don’t knock. They don’t ask if it’s okay. They come in, wag their tail, curl up in their bed, and fall asleep like they never left.

That’s a kind of trust we see often with dogs, but us? Not as often.

Life can feel like wandering. Like walking through places that aren’t home. Like seeking things that we thought was going to fill us with joy, and peace, and love, but does the complete opposite: leave us empty. Waiting for a voice to say, “You can stop running now.”

Dogs know something about that. They don’t worry about what they’ve done. They just run home.

Stop running in circles. Listen for His call. Go home, to the One who’s been waiting all along.

Final Thoughts The Walk Isn’t Over

If you’ve made it to the end of these lessons, thank you. That means you stayed. You listened. You walked a little further with us.

We didn’t write this book because we had all the answers. We wrote it because we’re still learning, too. Learning how to trust. How to rest. How to follow. How to stay close to the One who never leaves us.

The walk isn’t over yet. There’s more to learn. More to love. More to seek. But for now, we pray you find peace in the presence of the One who walks beside you.

Step by step. Wag by wag. Grace upon grace.

Some chapters may make you laugh. Others might stir something deeper. All of them are here to remind you that the way of Jesus is still possible, even in a world that often forgets Him.

So take your time. Read slowly. Or fast if you want to. But let these little stories settle into your mind and into your heart.

And remember: we don’t need to become louder to be faithful. We just need to stay close to the One who leads with truth, grace, and peace.

03-one-voice-one-leash-one-lord-005.book_title: One Voice, One Leash, One Lord

segments.v:
Politics, on the other hand, tends to sort people into camps: Us versus Them. Right versus. Wrong. Blue versus. Red. Win versus Lose. But people aren’t sides. People are souls, precious souls.

And many of them are wounded.

They’ve been hurt by words, or the lack of words, left out of conversations, judged by labels. Some… many don’t trust anymore. And some are just tired.

When Jesus walked the earth, He didn’t check credentials before healing. He didn’t quiz people on what they understood. He didn’t require that you be on this side or that side. He saw the heart first. He saw the wound — and moved toward it.

03-one-voice-one-leash-one-lord-038.book_title: One Voice, One Leash, One Lord

segments.v:
That’s what mercy does. It doesn’t mean we ignore the truth. It means we carry the truth but with gentleness and humility.

The world doesn’t need more shouting. It needs more wagging — small signs of welcome. A look that says, “You matter.” A tone that says, “You’re safe here, even if we disagree.”

Kindness won’t fix everything. But it opens the door. And sometimes, that’s all we need for healing to begin.

03-one-voice-one-leash-one-lord-039.book_title: One Voice, One Leash, One Lord

segments.v:
Loyalty that doesn’t shift with the headlines. Kindness that continues, even when people disagree. A voice that speaks truth, in love, and onlywhen it’s needed. And the courage to stand still when the fog is thick.

We’ve learned a lot from dogs in these pages. They don’t pretend to know everything. They’re not experts or influencers. But they know how to stay close by. They know how to listen, carefully. They know how to wait, sometimes for a long time. They know how to be welcoming. And some it seems know how to love.

03-one-voice-one-leash-one-lord-044.book_title: One Voice, One Leash, One Lord

segments.v:
The dog climbed into the car and settled in his spot. Tomorrow would be the same. 6:45 AM. Circle three times. Lie down. Be there.

Sometimes the most powerful ministry happens in the quietest faithfulness. Sometimes just showing up changes everything.

Scripture says the strong ought to bear with the weak. Tank lived it every day. He could have

42 dominated any space he entered. Instead, he made himself a shelter.

By closing time, the teenage cashier had handled her second Saturday successfully. As she counted down her register, Tank sat beside her, patient and protective.

“I used to be scared of big dogs,” she told the manager.

“Most people are. They see the size and assume danger.”

“But he’s the opposite,” the cashier said. “He makes me feel safe.”

After feeding him, she sat back at her desk. The emails had multiplied overnight. Dozens of new messages. Each one urgent.

Winston finished eating, walked to his bed in the sunny spot by the window, circled three times, and lay down. Within minutes, he was asleep again.

She looked at him fondly. “How do you do it? How can you sleep so much?”

But as she watched, she noticed something. When the doorbell rang with a delivery, Winston was instantly alert, checking who was there. When it was safe, he went back to sleep. When she got up to get coffee, he watched until she sat down, then closed his eyes again. He wasn’t lazy. He was conserving energy for when it mattered.

She hung up. Closed her laptop. Winston opened one eye, watching her.

“You’re right,” she told him. “Rest isn’t what you do when the work is done. It’s what you do so the work can get done.”

She lay down on the couch. Winston waddled over and curled up on the floor beside her.

For the first time in weeks, she slept. Really slept. She’d left the laptop at her desk, put her phone in airplane mode, and literally fell asleep within minutes.

A new client called, demanding a rush job over the weekend.

“I don’t work weekends,” she said.

“This is urgent!”

She looked at Winston, deeply asleep in his afternoon spot. “Everything’s urgent until it’s not. I’ll look at it Monday.”

“But—“

“Monday.”

She hung up. Winston’s tail thumped once against the floor, though his eyes stayed closed.

Scripture says, “Come to me, all who are weary and burdened, and I will give you rest.” Winston lived it. Rest wasn’t his reward for being a good dog. It was how he stayed a good dog.

The new vet, now mentoring the student, understood. In veterinary medicine, as in life, you’d make mistakes. Some small, some significant. The difference between those who thrived and those who burned out wasn’t skill or intelligence. It was the ability to accept grace: for yourself and others.

Honey aged over the years but never lost her ability to shake off the bad moments. Even on her last day at the clinic, when arthritis made her movements slow, she knocked over a water bowl, looked embarrassed for a moment, then wagged her tail and moved on.

The next morning, he made a phone call. He asked for help and admitted he couldn’t carry it alone.

Because sometimes the wisest thing we can do is stay with the pack. Even when it’s messy. Even when others move at different speeds. Even when we have to adjust our rhythm to match theirs.

Scripture says it plainly: “Two are better than one, because they have a good return for their labor. If either of them falls down, one can help the other up.”

The three dogs knew this without being told. They knew that what matters isn’t being the strongest or the fastest or the smartest. What matters is staying together, adjusting to each other’s pace, and carrying each other when the load gets too heavy.

Because together, we go farther than we ever could alone.

Final Thoughts Good business begins with a faithful heart.

Business is more than numbers and plans and products. It’s people. It’s choices. It’s moments where character is revealed and where trust is either built or broken.

Every deal, every deadline, every delay gives us a chance to show who we belong to.

The world tells us to compete, to climb, to hustle harder, to push past people and chase the win. But good dogs remind us of something simpler: faithfulness, loyalty, stillness, and trust.

They show up, they listen, they guard, they forgive, they follow. They stay close to us. And in those small, quiet acts, they point to something greater. Someone greater.

We walk with the same God who clothes the flowers and feeds the birds. He walks with us through boardrooms and job sites, offices and kitchens, early mornings and late nights.

God has not forgotten us. We are not left to carry this alone.

Whether we’re leading companies or folding towels, starting over or holding on, may we work with the kind of faith that trusts the Master, loves the pack, and stays ready to move when He calls.

The cold doesn’t care who you are. The cold affects everyone and doesn’t care if you were once a business owner, a teacher, a nurse, someone who worked for a big company, or a child. It wraps around anyone it finds—without kindness or memory. But God is not like the cold.
He will cover you with His feathers, and under His wings you will find refuge.
When the cold starts to freeze your veins, God comes near.

05-without-a-place-not-without-a-shepher.book_title: Without a Place, Not Without a Shepherd

segments.v: She gave more than all the others.

Why?

Because she gave from her need. Not her extra. She gave out of love.

There’s a myth we sometimes believe — that we need to have more than what we need before we can give. More money than we need. More time than we need. More security than we need. But the heart of God often works through what seems not much at all.
Five loaves and two fish.

05-without-a-place-not-without-a-shepher.book_title: Without a Place, Not Without a Shepherd

segments.v:
That’s all one boy had. And it was more than enough for thousands — when placed in the hands of Jesus.

Maybe all you have today is half a sandwich. Maybe just a kind word. Maybe a quiet moment to sit beside someone who is struggling, in pain, and suffering.

It might not feel like much. But in a world where people are passed over who have nothing, even crumbs can taste like a feast.
Whoever gives even a cup of cold water to one of these little ones… will surely not lose their reward.

05-without-a-place-not-without-a-shepher.book_title: Without a Place, Not Without a Shepherd

segments.v:
A man used to say, “You’re never too poor to give something. Even if it’s just your place in line.”

Because sometimes the greatest generosity isn’t what we give — but how we give it. Give without fear. Give without keeping score. Give without waiting to have more. With open hands. And an open heart.

05-without-a-place-not-without-a-shepher.book_title: Without a Place, Not Without a Shepherd

segments.v:
We live in a world that often ties worth to paperwork. To housing status. To appearance. To whether we have a place to return to. But grace doesn’t require credentials.

Jesus never said, “Show Me your résumé.” He said:
Come to Me, all who are weary and burdened, and I will give you rest.

All. No collar required.

There was a man who spent months trying to get his ID back. He’d lost it in the shuffle — a shelter here, a hospital there, a bag stolen while he was asleep on the sidewalk. He told us, “It’s like the world forgot I exist.” But then someone called him by name. Someone said, “You’re on the list.” And he wept.

05-without-a-place-not-without-a-shepher.book_title: Without a Place, Not Without a Shepherd

segments.v:
There’s something about steps that keep going even when they hurt. Something holy about a journey that no one else sees — but God does.

One man said, “I used to think I had to clean myself up before I could be worth anything again. But now I think — maybe the fact that I’m still walking is the miracle.” Still walking. It is a miracle indeed.

Sometimes we think healing has to be quick. That faith has to look strong. That only progress counts if it’s measurable.

05-without-a-place-not-without-a-shepher.book_title: Without a Place, Not Without a Shepherd

segments.v: But the Shepherd sees every step. Even the shaky ones. Even the ones that double back. Even the ones where we fall.
The steps of a good man are ordered by the Lord, and He delights in his way. Though he fall, he shall not be utterly cast down, for the Lord upholds him with His hand.
You might not be where you hoped you’d be by now. You might have taken more wrong turns than you can count. But if you’re still trying — still walking — God hasn’t given up on you.

05-without-a-place-not-without-a-shepher.book_title: Without a Place, Not Without a Shepherd

segments.v: The prodigal son didn’t run home. He limped. He had dust on his feet, shame on his shoulders, a speech in his mouth he never had to finish. Because even though he was still far away, his father had already run out to meet him.
While he was still a long way off…

God doesn’t wait at the finish line. He meets us on the road.

That old dog eventually stopped walking. But not because he gave up. He stopped because someone opened their door. They said, “You’ve been looking for something. You don’t have to anymore. You’re home.”

05-without-a-place-not-without-a-shepher.book_title: Without a Place, Not Without a Shepherd

segments.v: Every step you've taken — yes, even the wrong ones — have brought you here. And He's still walking with you. Every mile. Every moment. You are not lost. You are on your way.

05-without-a-place-not-without-a-shepher.book_title: Without a Place, Not Without a Shepherd

segments.v:
She found what she’d been looking for. Safety. Food. Consistency. Care. In other words — home.

We often think of “home” as a roof and four walls. And it can be. But there’s another kind of home. The kind we carry. The kind we find when we are known, loved, and not sent away. That kind of home doesn’t depend on real estate. It depends on relationships.
Surely Your goodness and mercy will follow me all the days of my life, and I will dwell in the house of the Lord forever.

05-without-a-place-not-without-a-shepher.book_title: Without a Place, Not Without a Shepherd

segments.v:
He smiled. Then added, “I think God might’ve been following me this whole time.”

Jesus didn’t come to give us only shelter. He came to give us Himself.
Remain in me, and I will remain in you.

Abide. Remain. Stay. Dwell. That’s home.

Maybe you’re still searching. Maybe your life has been filled with too many goodbyes, too many doors that didn’t open, too many nights alone.

But the Shepherd never left. He’s been walking beside you, even when you didn’t know it. And today, He’s not just offering a place. He’s offering Himself.

05-without-a-place-not-without-a-shepher.book_title: Without a Place, Not Without a Shepherd

segments.v: I am the good shepherd. The good shepherd lays down His life for the sheep.

You don’t have to earn your way into His flock. You can’t. You don’t have to fix everything first. You can’t. You don’t even have to know where you’re going. Because He knows. And He’s already moving towards you. Actually, He’s right there beside you.

If today you’re without a place, please know — you are not without a Shepherd. You are not forgotten. You are not alone. Not now. Not ever.

05-without-a-place-not-without-a-shepher.book_title: Without a Place, Not Without a Shepherd

segments.v:
Max reminds us that enthusiasm isn’t about having perfect circumstances. It’s about choosing to be excited about the possibilities each new day brings. When we wake up with genuine eagerness for what God might do in and through us today, everything changes.

The apostle Paul understood this. Even while imprisoned, he wrote letters overflowing with joy and excitement about God’s work. He wasn’t faking it or trying to be positive for the sake of it. He was genuinely enthusiastic about being part of God’s story.
This is the day the Lord has made; let us rejoice and be glad in it.

David didn’t write this on his best day. He wrote it knowing that every day, including rainy days, boring days, and difficult days, comes from God’s hands. When we start each morning remembering that God has given us this specific day for a specific purpose, we can wake up with Max-level enthusiasm.

Teenage years aren’t a waiting room for real life to begin. They’re not a dress rehearsal. God has work for us to do, people for us to love, and growth for us to experience right now, today. That’s worth getting excited about.

So tomorrow morning, before checking our phone or thinking about everything there is to do, try waking up like Max. Thank God for giving us another day. Ask Him to help us see the possibilities instead of just the problems. Choose enthusiasm over apathy.

After all, if a dog can be that excited about the same kibble and the same backyard every single day, imagine how thrilled we can be about serving the God who creates new mercies every morning.

Buddy has been doing this for eight years now. Through Sarah’s awkward middle school phase, her

32 parents’ stressful financial year, her grandmother’s death, and countless ordinary days that weren’t particularly good or bad. He’s never once called in sick to his job of being loyal. He’s never threatened to find a more exciting family. He just shows up, day after day, offering the same steady presence and unconditional love.

In a world that celebrates flashy achievements and dramatic gestures, Buddy represents something equally valuable but often overlooked: faithfulness. He reminds us that the most important things in life aren’t the spectacular moments that make great stories; they’re the consistent, daily choices to love and serve others.

Faithfulness means showing up even when we don’t feel like it. It means keeping our word even when it’s inconvenient. It means loving people through their difficult seasons, not just their fun ones. It means doing our homework, honoring our parents, and

34 treating friends well even when no one is watching or applauding.

It also means being faithful to God. Not just during exciting spiritual highs, but during ordinary weeks when faith feels more like discipline than emotion. Reading our Bible even when it feels dry. Praying even when heaven feels silent. Choosing integrity even when cutting corners would be easier.

If you’re a teenager, you've probably been told that rest is basically laziness in disguise. The message from school, social media, college prep culture, everywhere is that you should always be doing something productive. Taking a break means falling behind. Relaxing means you’re not serious about success.

But Walter suggests a different approach. He reminds us that rest isn’t the opposite of productivity. Rest is what makes productivity possible. A tired dog is an ineffective dog. A tired teenager is an ineffective teenager.

— Matthew 11:28-29

This invitation from Jesus isn’t just about spiritual rest (though it includes that). It’s about the deep soul-rest that comes from knowing we don’t have to earn God’s love through our achievements. We don’t have to prove our worth through constant activity. We can rest because our value is already established.

Even God rested. After creating the universe, He took a Sabbath day, not because He was tired, but because rest is built into the rhythm of healthy life. If the Creator of everything took time to rest, maybe we should too.

Rest isn’t being selfish; it’s being a good caretaker. Taking care of our physical and emotional energy means we’ll have more to give to others. Getting enough sleep, taking breaks, saying no to some activities aren’t signs of weakness. They’re signs of wisdom.

This doesn’t mean we should sleep sixteen hours a day like Walter (though teenagers do need more sleep than adults). It means building healthy rhythms of work and rest into our lives. It means recognizing that downtime isn’t wasted time; it’s recovery time.

Prioritize sleep. Our brain literally needs it to function properly. Those all-nighters might seem necessary, but they usually end up not helping.

Take real breaks. Put the phone down. Step away from homework. Sit outside. Let our mind wander.

Practice saying no. We don’t have to participate in every activity or accept every invitation. It’s okay to have times when we do nothing in our schedule.

Don’t feel guilty about rest. We’re not being lazy when we take care of ourselves. We’re being responsible.

Find our own version of Walter’s sunny spots. Maybe it’s reading for pleasure, taking walks, listening to music, or just sitting quietly. Figure out what actually restores us, not just what distracts us.

Walter doesn’t apologize for his naps because he knows they’re necessary. We don’t have to apologize

40 for taking care of ourselves either. In a world that makes us believe that being super tired is a great thing, choosing rest is actually a radical act of faith: trusting that our worth isn’t measured by our output.

When our life falls apart, when we lose our job, our marriage, our reputation, everything feels scarce. Money, opportunities, support, hope. It all seems less now. We’re living with smaller portions of everything we once took for granted.

In this scarcity, we face Tank’s choice. Do we hoard what little we have left? Do we pull back from helping others because we don’t have enough for ourselves? Do we use our crisis as an excuse to become selfish? Or do we become more generous, not less?
All they asked was that we should continue to remember the poor, the very thing I had been eager to do.

Paul wrote this while describing a meeting with church leaders about his ministry. Even though Paul was facing persecution, financial hardship, and constant danger, the one thing the leaders emphasized was continuing to help others who had even less.

Notice they didn’t say “help the poor when your life is stable” or “be generous when you have plenty.” They said continue to remember the poor even when you’re struggling, even when your own resources are limited, even when it would be understandable to focus only on your own survival.

True generosity isn’t giving when we have excess. It’s sharing when we have little.

Tank’s generosity meant more during the rationing than it would have during abundance. When food was plentiful, sharing was easy. When it was limited, sharing was costly. And therefore more meaningful.

Our generosity means more now, in our season of loss and limitation, than it did when we had plenty. When people see us sharing our limited resources, caring for others despite our own struggles, choosing to help even when we need help ourselves… That reveals character that abundance never could.

The person who gives generously when they’re wealthy might just have good financial planning. The person who shares their last twenty dollars with someone hungrier than they are? That’s someone whose heart has been changed by understanding what it means to receive mercy.

Let others go first: in conversations, in opportunities, in receiving help or attention. Use your strength, whatever form it takes, to create space for those who are smaller or weaker.

If or when abundance returns to your life, remember the lessons you learned during rationing. Character built in scarcity doesn’t have to disappear in prosperity. Let your current difficulties reveal the best parts of who you are, not the worst.

Rosie’s fourteen months of waiting weren’t wasted time; they were preparation time. Every skill she learned, every improvement in her behavior, every choice to hope instead of despair was making her ready for Sergeant Martinez who would eventually return.

Our waiting periods aren’t dead time either. They’re development time. The patience we’re learning, the character we’re building, the skills we’re developing while we wait… these are preparing us for the opportunities that are coming.

Rosie couldn’t rush the return of Sergeant Martinez, but she could be ready when it happened. We can’t

Good waiting produces character. Shortcuts do not. Our extended waiting period is developing qualities in us that instant restoration couldn’t create.

Wait. He is preparing us.

Don’t rush your healing process. Emotional and spiritual healing from major mistakes often takes longer than we expect and requires patience with the process.

Use your experience of receiving extra care to become someone who gives extra care to others who are broken. Your healing can prepare you to help others who are going through similar pain.

Needing intensive care doesn’t mean you’re weak or defective. It means you’re valuable enough to invest in. God doesn’t give His most careful attention to

We didn’t write this book because we have all the answers. We wrote it because we’re still learning, too. Still learning how to trust when it feels impossible to trust. Still learning how to hope when it feels foolish to hope. Still learning how to believe we’re loved when we feel unlovable.

Dogs have taught us more than we expected. Not through big moments or dramatic rescues. But in the small and quiet ones. In the way they wait without giving up. In the way they love without keeping score. In the way they forgive quickly and completely. In the way they see the good in people even when those people can’t see it in themselves.

Until then, let’s keep wagging our tails even when it feels foolish. Keep believing that we are loved. We are

74 valuable because God values us. Know that we are going home because God will take us there.

We are all damaged. But we are all still loved by God. And that makes all the difference.

Whoever you are, whatever brought you here, we want you to know: these struggles are real, they matter, and you’re not alone in them.

The stories ahead won’t provide easy answers. Family dynamics, cultural heritage, and generational expectations are complex realities that can’t be solved in simple steps. But we hope these twelve lessons will offer something just as valuable: recognition, understanding, and the beginning of freedom.

Because here’s what we’ve discovered: the very things that make family heritage beautiful, the traditions, values, stories, and dreams passed down through generations, can also become prisons

The girl understood then what she was seeing in her dog. Visitors judged him by his perfect behavior during their stay, by how well he performed and how impressive he appeared. But that performance was exhausting and false. The

16 real dog, the one with his true heart showing, only appeared when no one demanded anything of him.

She wondered if people experienced the same thing. Perhaps they learned to perform for approval, showing only the version of themselves that others expected to see. Perhaps they became exhausted from constantly acting perfect, never able to simply be themselves without judgment.

The contrast was clear: the dog performing for approval versus the dog simply being. One required constant effort and drained him of joy. The other was natural and restful. Her dog’s worth was not in his perfect sit-stay or his controlled greeting of guests. His value was in his actual self, the one that played and loved freely and sometimes missed the tennis ball entirely. That was the dog she loved. That was the heart that mattered.

His wife put her hand on his shoulder. “It’s still hard.”

“Yes.”

He nodded. The ministry was slower than he’d hoped. He’d had to say no to some travel so he could be home for family dinners. He’d told village elders he was still learning, still making mistakes, and still figuring out how to live in a culture that wasn’t his own.

But his family was getting better. And that, he was learning, was ministry too. It was maybe the most important kind.

The father would sometimes find himself watching his son study at the kitchen table.

“You know what, mijo?” he’d say. “I think this is exactly where you’re supposed to be.”

A Pause for Breath

If you’ve made it this far, you might need a moment to breathe.

We understand. These first six stories aren’t easy ones.

You’ve read about dogs who learned to perform on command and families who forgot how to simply be together. You’ve seen young people carrying burdens they never asked for, trying to meet standards someone else set for them.

Maybe you recognized yourself in one of these stories. Maybe in several. Or maybe you’re holding this book because someone you love is struggling, and you’re only beginning to understand why.

Wherever you are, we want you to know that you’re not alone in what you’re feeling.

Dogs know something about rest. After they’ve been running hard or working all day, they just stop. They find a spot, lie down, and close their eyes. They don’t feel guilty about needing a break.

Under the table, both dogs lay near each other. Atlas’s massive form took up most of the space. Buddy was curled up against him, snoring softly.

“The father didn’t love one son more than the other,” the father explained. “Both sons were lost in different ways. One was lost out in the world.

The other was lost right there at home, thinking he had to earn love by being good.”

The son looked at his sister. “I’m sorry,” he said suddenly. “For being the good one all the time. For making it harder for you.”

“He’s our dog now,” the mother said. And for the first time, it felt true.

The daughter had started unpacking her room, finally. “Not because I think we’re staying,” she told her mother. “But because wherever we are, we should actually live there. Not just exist waiting for the next move.”

The son joined the base basketball team. He hadn’t forgotten his friends from before. But he had realized that you could honor what was lost while still engaging with what was present.
