If you have made it through all twelve lessons, thank you for staying with us. Thank you for walking through these stories of dogs and families trying to figure out what really matters.

We did not write this book because we have answers. We wrote it because we have walked similar paths ourselves. We know what it feels like to carry expectations that seem too heavy. We know the exhaustion that comes from always being “on.” And we know the relief that comes when someone finally sees us for who we are instead of what we represent.

In the story of the prodigal son, Jesus described a father who ran to embrace his returning child before that child had accomplished anything, proven anything, or earned anything. The father’s love was not a reward for success. It was simply there, waiting, unconditional.

And in the same story, Jesus described an older brother who had done everything right. He had followed every rule, met every expectation, and never strayed from the family path. But he was so focused on performance that he missed the celebration happening right in front of him.

Many of us are like that older brother. We work so hard to earn love that we forget it was never something we had to earn in the first place.

Heritage is a gift. Family traditions can be beautiful. Cultural values connect us to something larger than ourselves. But when these things become the only measure of our worth, when they leave no room for our individual calling, when they demand perfection rather than growth, they can crush the very people they were meant to bless.

So take a breath. Let your shoulders drop. You do not have to perform today. You do not have to represent anyone but yourself. You are not a brand to be managed or a legacy to be protected. You are a person, a child of God, deeply loved.

And that love is available to you right now, exactly as you are.

Marriage is the most important relationship most of us will ever have. It is also the most difficult. Not because we married the wrong person, but because marriage asks us to do the hardest things: stay when it would be easier to leave, speak when silence feels safer, forgive when the wound is still open, and trust again when trust has been broken.

The dogs in this book did not solve any of these problems. Dogs cannot. But they showed us things about ourselves that we could not see on our own. They held up a mirror we did not ask for, and the reflection was uncomfortable, and it was true.

He had always thought that story was about Mary making the right choice. But sitting in the kitchen, looking at his wife asleep on the couch, he wondered if it was really about Martha. About the kind of person who cannot stop running. About how sometimes the most loving thing someone can say to that person is: sit down. I am here. You do not have to hold everything together tonight.

He walked back to the living room. His wife was still asleep. He did not wake her.

He had been carrying the branch alone. And just like Teddy, he could not get it through the gate. It was too big for one person. It had always been too big for one person.

He thought about a verse he had read once, back when things were easier and it had seemed like advice for other people. *Carry each other’s burdens, and in this way you will fulfill the law of Christ.* He had always read that verse as something he should do for others. He had never considered that it might be asking him to let someone do it for him.

We believe marriage was designed by God. Not as a reward for getting life right, but as a place where two imperfect people learn what it means to love the way God loves: without giving up.

If you are reading this in the middle of a difficult marriage, we want you to know that we see you. We do not have advice. We do not have a formula. We have twelve dogs who showed us twelve truths, and every one of those truths pointed to the same God who made marriage and who stays in it with us, even when we cannot stay in it with each other.

He does not pick up His phone when you are talking. He does not close the door. He does not leave for something better. He hears your voice, and He responds. He holds the cord when your hands are too tired to hold it.

We are two ordinary people: a deacon and a layperson. We are still learning. Still failing. Still getting back up. Still amazed that God has not given up on us or on the people we married.

“Buddy doesn’t try to fix anything,” Daniel continued. “He doesn’t tell me it’s all going to be okay or that I should look on the bright side. He just stays with me. That helps more than all the advice.”

As James reflected on Daniel’s words, he began to understand something important about ministry. So often, church leaders feel pressure to have the right words, the perfect Scripture verse, or the solution to people’s problems. But maybe the most powerful ministry wasn’t always about what you said or did. Maybe sometimes it was simply about showing up and staying close.

When the choir director’s elderly mother was dying, James didn’t try to explain God’s timing or offer comfort through theological reasoning. He just checked in regularly and asked practical questions about what kind of support would be most helpful.

James learned that you don’t need a seminary degree to minister to people. You don’t need perfect words or profound insights. Sometimes the most meaningful ministry is simply being willing to enter into someone else’s pain and stay there with them, just like Buddy had done for Tom.

Six months after Maya’s death, Daniel stopped by James’s office at the church.

“I wanted to thank you,” Daniel said. “For all those times you just sat with me. You didn’t try to make everything better or give me easy answers. You just stayed. That meant more than you know.”

James smiled, thinking of Buddy’s faithful presence during those difficult weeks. He had learned something important from that old dog: sometimes the best thing you can offer someone is simply to show up and stay with them.

Daniel’s eyes grew a little misty. “You know, Maya and Buddy had such a special bond. During all those months of treatments, he never left her side. And she would talk to him like he was her best friend. I think they understood each other in a way that went beyond words.”

James realized that Buddy had taught him one of the most important lessons about church ministry: presence is often more powerful than words, and staying close to people in their pain is sometimes the most Christ-like thing you can do.

Dogs are good at just being near us.

They don’t try to cheer us up with advice. They don’t leave when things get awkward or strange. They simply stay. A dog that quietly stays with us is a gift, especially when we’re hurting.

I remember sitting beside someone in a hospital once. There wasn’t much I could do. There was absolutely nothing I could do. No way to make the situation better. No perfect words. Not one. Just a chair and a little bit of time. I sat. I stayed. I prayed in my heart. And when I got up to leave, he said quietly, “Thanks for being here.”

Sometimes what people need most isn’t a solution. It’s not the right words or an action plan. It’s just someone who doesn’t walk away.

Jesus did that.

He stayed with people who were hurting. He touched the ones everyone else avoided. He cried with His friends. He didn’t run from pain. Not their pain, and not ours.

The Bible says, “The Lord is close to the brokenhearted.” It doesn’t say He fixes everything right away. It says He’s close.

Don’t we all want to be like that? Stay close when someone feels alone. Even if we can’t fix the situation. Even if we don’t know what to say. Even if we can’t do anything to make things better.

Dogs don’t always understand what we’re going through. But they know when to stay close. That’s a kind of love. Not just showing up when it’s easy, but sticking around when it is difficult.

We don’t have to have all the answers. We don’t need answers at all. When things get hard, it’s easy to disappear, but if we want to love we need to stick around. We need to stay, just like the Lord stays with us, especially through hard times.

Dogs have taught us more than we expected.

Not through big moments, but in the small ones. In the way they wait. In the way they stay close. In the way they forgive easily and love without holding back.

We believe God does that, too. Only deeper. Only truer.

And if you’re reading this while walking through something hard such as sickness, loss, fear, loneliness we hope these simple stories reminded you of something real:

You are not forgotten. You are not too far gone. And you are never, ever alone.
Jesus knew what it was like to be without a place.
Foxes have dens and birds have nests, but the Son of Man has no place to lay his head.

The Son of God walked this earth with nowhere to settle. He belonged everywhere and nowhere at once. And yet He came — for us. He gave up everything for us, for the overlooked, for those still searching for home.

One man shared:

“I’m not just homeless. I’m human. I lost my house, not my heart.”

05-without-a-place-not-without-a-shepher.book_title: Without a Place, Not Without a Shepherd

segments.v:
This verse isn’t just about clothes. It’s about seeing people — the way Jesus sees them — especially when the world turns away.

Maybe you know what it feels like to be cold. Not just your skin, but your soul. Maybe life has numbed you. Maybe you’ve been brushed past or spoken over. Maybe you’re still trying to warm up after a long winter of loss.

Please know this:
The Lord is close to the brokenhearted.
The cold may not care. But the Shepherd does.

05-without-a-place-not-without-a-shepher.book_title: Without a Place, Not Without a Shepherd

segments.v: But here’s what Scripture says:
Though my father and mother forsake me, the Lord will receive me.

When everyone else walks away, God does not. God draws closer. He does not leave when things get hard. He does not pull back when we are messed up, weak, or not who we used to be. He stays, close to us. And He carries us through.
Even to your old age and gray hairs I am He, I am He who will sustain you. I have made you and I will carry you.

05-without-a-place-not-without-a-shepher.book_title: Without a Place, Not Without a Shepherd

segments.v:
Maybe you’ve been left behind. Maybe by family, friends, or by your church. Maybe by a system that should have helped. Maybe by the version of life you once hoped for.

But remember, you’re not forgotten. Not to God. He sees where you are hurting. He knows what you’ve lost. And He’s not waiting for you to be better. He moves toward you. Actually, He’s already here. God is saying to us, “Come to me. Come home.” God’s door is always open. All we have to do is open our own hearts, walk through, and be embraced by God. And we are home.

05-without-a-place-not-without-a-shepher.book_title: Without a Place, Not Without a Shepherd

segments.v: Because of the Lord’s great love, we are not consumed, for His compassion never fails. They are new every morning; great is Your faithfulness.

The prophet Jeremiah wrote these words during the darkest period of his life. His nation was destroyed, his people were in exile, and everything he had worked for was in ruins. He had every reason to believe God had abandoned him.

But in the middle of his despair, Jeremiah remembered this truth: God’s compassion never fails.

When our life breaks apart from our mistakes, we often assume that our brokenness makes us less valuable to others and to God. We think that admitting our pain, showing our wounds, or acknowledging our need for help will drive people away. We try to appear strong and put-together when we’re actually falling apart inside.

But sometimes our most broken moments are when we become most precious to the people who truly love us.

“The Lord is close to the brokenhearted and saves those who are crushed in spirit.”

— Psalm 34:18

David wrote this after experiencing deep personal pain and public failure. He discovered that God doesn’t maintain emotional distance from people who are falling apart. Instead, God moves closer to us when we’re most broken, most vulnerable, most in need of intensive care.

The word ‘close’ here means intimate, personal, nearby. When our heart is shattered by the consequences of our actions, guilt over our choices, or grief over what we’ve lost, God doesn’t observe our pain from a safe distance. He gets close enough to touch our wounds.

‘Saves’ doesn’t just mean rescue from danger. It means heal, restore, make whole again. God’s response to our brokenness isn’t just to pull us out of trouble, but to carefully rehabilitate what’s been damaged.

Bella’s injury didn’t make her less adoptable. It revealed how much love was available to her when she needed it most. The same people who’d given her routine care became passionately invested in her healing when they saw how much she was hurting.

Our brokenness from major mistakes works similarly. The people who truly love us don’t distance themselves when we’re wounded. They move closer.

Our pain doesn’t make us less valuable to God; it makes us the focus of His most intensive care.

Bella received more personal attention during her eight weeks of recovery than she’d gotten in her previous year of good health. Sometimes our seasons of brokenness become our seasons of experiencing the deepest love.

Look for evidence of “intensive care” in your life. People who are showing you extra kindness during this difficult season. Unexpected sources of help and comfort. Moments when you feel especially cared for despite your circumstances.

Your broken heart is precious to Him. When you're tempted to hide your pain or tough it out alone, remember that those who are hurting get extra care, not less care. Your wounds don't make you less valuable. They make you the focus of the most tender love available.
And if I go and prepare a place for you, I will come back and take you to be with me that you also may be where I am.

Jesus spoke these words to His disciples the night before He was killed. He was about to go through the worst experience of His life. But He wanted them to know that wasn’t the end of the story. He was going ahead to prepare for their reunion.

Scout pressed against his side, the dog’s head resting on his chest.

“I’m sorry,” the boy whispered, tears streaming down his face. “I’m so sorry I let you down.”

His grandmother, who had been staying with them during the tournament, sat in her corner chair with her worn Spanish Bible. She had been reading the same passage over and over since they had returned from the hospital.

“Mijo,” she said softly, “listen to this. ‘Man looks at the outward appearance, but the Lord looks at the heart.’” She closed the Bible gently. “God doesn’t need you to be a star to love you. Neither do we.”

She lay down on her stomach and made herself small. “Your person is gone. I get it. My best friend feels gone too, even though she’s just far away. But maybe home isn’t about places or even specific people. Maybe it’s about knowing you’re loved wherever you are.”

Slowly, carefully, she started humming a quiet hymn from church. “Be still my soul, the Lord is on thy side…”

Jax’s ears turned toward her voice.

“Bear patiently the cross of grief or pain…”

If you are reading this because your marriage is difficult right now, we want you to know something before you begin: you are not alone. We have been where you are. We have sat in quiet houses wondering what happened. We have said things we cannot take back. We have been hurt by the people closest to us, and we have been the ones who did the hurting.

If you are reading this because your marriage is already over, we want you to know that this book is for you, too. These stories are not just for people who stayed. They are for anyone who has loved someone and been changed by it, for better or for worse.

The cord had held. Not because they were strong enough to hold it. Because God was.

Final Thoughts

If you have made it to the end of these twelve lessons, thank you for reading. This was not an easy book to read. It was not an easy book to write.

Marriage is the closest thing most of us will ever experience to the way God loves us: completely, permanently, and at great cost. It is also the place where we do the most damage, because it is the place where we are the most known. You cannot deeply wound a stranger. You can only deeply wound someone who let you in.

We did not write this book because our marriages are perfect. We wrote it because they are not. We have been the husband who picks up his phone at the dinner table. We have been the wife who stops asking questions she already knows the answer to. We have sat on the couch in a quiet house and wondered how two people who chose each other ended up so far apart.

Dogs have shown us things we were not ready to see.

If you are reading this and your marriage is already over, we see you too. Maybe you did everything you could and it was not enough. Maybe you carry guilt for the ways you did not. Maybe you are somewhere in between, still trying to make sense of what happened. We want you to know that God does not love you less. The cord between you and Him has not been cut. He is close to the brokenhearted, and that includes hearts broken by divorce, by separation, by the slow and painful end of something you thought would last forever. These stories are for you, too. Every one of them.

We hope these stories reminded you that you are not alone in your marriage, even when it feels like you are. God is close to the brokenhearted. He saves those who are crushed in spirit. And He is very, very good at holding things together.

The cord of three strands is not quickly broken.

Hold on.

What’s most amazing is that Yuki shows up simply because that’s what faithful friends do. She doesn’t ask for treats, praise, or attention. Her reward is the relationship itself.

This faithful presence has become such a big part of the Chen family routine that they only notice it when it’s missing. On the rare times when Yuki is at the veterinarian or staying with relatives, Mr. Chen finds himself looking down at his feet during his evening reading time. The space feels empty.

Yuki’s approach to faithfulness is beautiful in its simplicity. She can’t understand most problems. Most challenges are too big for her. She just shows up, stays close, and remains available. By doing that, she is saying: “You’re not alone. I’m here. I’m not going anywhere.”

After years of this evening routine, it becomes clear that the biggest gift Yuki gives isn’t entertainment or protection from danger; it’s the security of knowing that she cares enough to show up every day, day after day.

Maybe that’s what God wants from us too. The Bible says that real religion, the kind God cares about, is looking after orphans and widows. It’s about helping those who can’t help themselves. It’s about caring for people most others ignore.

People in shelters and on sidewalks don’t have much, but they still look out for one another. They share food. Sometimes they even take turns keeping watch at night. They remember who’s sick, who’s scared, who needs an extra blanket.

Jesus did the same.

He was always stopping for the ones no one noticed: the blind man calling from the side of the road, the woman who touched His robe in a crowd, the children the disciples tried to keep quiet.

Jesus saw them. He saw their hearts. He stood with them. He shared in their suffering. He guarded them, even when others didn’t.

Let’s be more like that. Let’s be someone who sees those who are invisible to the world. Someone who steps in to protect those who are weak. Someone who says, “Not this one. Not today.”

He greeted everyone with a wag. Didn’t matter who you were, or how your day had gone. If you walked through that door, you were met with kindness.

That wag wasn’t just excitement. Yes, he was excited, but it was also a welcome.

There’s something healing about being seen, welcomed — not for what you’ve done, not for what you believe, not for how smart you are or how much you have, but simply because you’re human. Dogs seem to understand that much better than we do.

03-one-voice-one-leash-one-lord-037.book_title: One Voice, One Leash, One Lord

segments.v:
Instead, she’d trained him to read situations. To position himself between threat and vulnerable. To use his size as a shield, not a weapon.

A month earlier, during inventory, an elderly employee had fallen in the stockroom. Tank had immediately lain down beside him, letting the man use his solid body to sit up, then stand. Tank stayed quiet and still, being what was needed: something stable to lean on.

“People see a Rottweiler and think violence,” the manager told the cashier as they closed that evening. “But real strength isn’t about attacking. It’s about knowing when someone needs protection and quietly providing it.”
And isn’t that what we all hope for?
I have called you by name. You are mine.

God sees who we are and whose we are. Even without a place, we are not without a Shepherd.

The dog didn’t ask for much. A name. A little food. A place to lie down. And someone to stay with.

That’s all most people need, too. And that’s what the Shepherd gives.

05-without-a-place-not-without-a-shepher.book_title: Without a Place, Not Without a Shepherd

segments.v:
You might be reading this and feel like no one sees you. Like you’ve been passed by more times than you can count. Maybe your life feels like a mess. Maybe you’ve been hurt by people who were supposed to help. Please hear this: God has never walked past you. Not once. He sees you. He stops. And He stays.

And if you’ve been the one to stop — maybe in a small way, maybe with tears in your own eyes — thank you.

05-without-a-place-not-without-a-shepher.book_title: Without a Place, Not Without a Shepherd

segments.v:
Every time we pause for someone in pain, every time we sit and wait without judgment, every time we let our hearts be interrupted, we echo the heart of Jesus.

Some dogs survive because someone stopped. Some people do, too. And maybe that’s part of how the Shepherd works. Through the slow hands of those who are willing to stop. To listen. To love.

05-without-a-place-not-without-a-shepher.book_title: Without a Place, Not Without a Shepherd

segments.v:
That was years ago. But he still sat there — every afternoon — just in case.

Faith doesn’t always look bold. Sometimes it looks like sitting, still. Like hoping when everyone else tells you to give up. Like believing love remembers you — even when you’ve been left in silence.

Jesus told a story about that kind of waiting.
But while he was still a long way off, his father saw him and was filled with compassion for him; he ran to his son…

05-without-a-place-not-without-a-shepher.book_title: Without a Place, Not Without a Shepherd

segments.v:
The prodigal son had wandered far. But the father had never stopped watching the road. That’s what love does. It waits. It looks out the window. It keeps a light on.

There’s a deeper kind of waiting, too. The kind that says: “Even if the person I long for never returns… I will still be ready to forgive. To welcome. To try again.”

A woman once told us, “I’ve waited my whole life for someone to come back. But somewhere along the way, I realized… it’s God who’s been waiting for me.” That shift in perspective, toward the truth of God, the heart of God — that quiet awakening — changed her.

05-without-a-place-not-without-a-shepher.book_title: Without a Place, Not Without a Shepherd

segments.v: Not all waiting ends with a reunion. But all waiting can lead us back to the One who never stopped hoping for us.
The Lord is good to those who wait for Him, to the soul who seeks Him.

The little dog on the side of the road hadn’t moved in hours. People said he’d been sitting there since morning. Someone must have promised to come back. And maybe they didn’t. But that dog still believed.

05-without-a-place-not-without-a-shepher.book_title: Without a Place, Not Without a Shepherd

segments.v: Some people only see the outside. The tangled hair. Rips in the clothes. Or the broken places. They see what’s missing. What’s wrong. What doesn’t look clean or safe or presentable. But God sees deeper.
The Lord does not look at the things people look at. People look at the outward appearance, but the Lord looks at the heart.
There was a man who had lost most of his teeth. His skin was worn, and his eyes were tired. People avoided him in public. He told us, “Sometimes I go days without hearing my own name.”

05-without-a-place-not-without-a-shepher.book_title: Without a Place, Not Without a Shepherd

segments.v:
But one afternoon, a child came in with a meal for the shelter. And that child looked him in the eyes, smiled wide, and said, “Hi.” That child had no fear. He had no hesitation. All he had was kindness. He said later, “That one moment reminded me I’m still a person.”

Jesus did that all the time. He touched lepers. He dined with outcasts. He called people by name when others called them unclean. He never saw people as problems to be fixed — but as souls to be loved.

05-without-a-place-not-without-a-shepher.book_title: Without a Place, Not Without a Shepherd

segments.v:
A volunteer at a shelter once made it a habit to learn everyone’s name. Even if someone had only been there one night, even if they didn’t want to talk, she’d remember. One man said, “You know what makes this place feel different? They don’t just serve soup. They remember and say my name.”

We were never meant to live as nameless bodies moving through the world. We were created to be known — by God and by one another.

05-without-a-place-not-without-a-shepher.book_title: Without a Place, Not Without a Shepherd

segments.v: Fear not, for I have redeemed you; I have called you by name; you are mine.

There is power in being called by your name. In being reminded that you are not invisible. Not forgotten. Not “just another homeless man.” Not “just a stray.” But someone. Someone worth remembering.

One day Jesus passed through a crowd and stopped beneath a tree. There was a man hiding there — a tax collector named Zacchaeus, hated and avoided by many. But Jesus didn’t scold him. He said:

05-without-a-place-not-without-a-shepher.book_title: Without a Place, Not Without a Shepherd

segments.v: Zacchaeus, come down immediately. I must stay at your house today.

He said his name. That was the beginning of Zacchaeus’ healing. Not the end of the story — but the start of coming home to God.

Maybe you’ve gone a long time without hearing your name in a gentle voice. Maybe you’ve been called things that have hurt you. Maybe you’ve been overlooked, passed by, or treated like you don’t matter. But the Shepherd knows your name. And even now He is calling your name.

05-without-a-place-not-without-a-shepher.book_title: Without a Place, Not Without a Shepherd

segments.v: Sometimes it’s not the big gestures that carry the most weight. It’s a bowl of water on a hot day. It’s someone remembering you like you matter. It’s being called “friend,” or “sir,” or “ma’am,” when the world expects no one to look you in the eye.
And if anyone gives even a cup of cold water to one of these little ones because he is My disciple, truly I tell you, he will never lose his reward.

05-without-a-place-not-without-a-shepher.book_title: Without a Place, Not Without a Shepherd

segments.v: Not because of the paperwork. But because someone still saw him as someone worth counting.
I will not forget you. See, I have engraved you on the palms of My hands.

Even when the world loses track of us, God does not. Even if we’re undocumented, displaced, or unnamed, He knows exactly where we are — and to Him, we are not missing. We are beloved.

The little dog with no collar? She never left. She didn’t need an owner’s permission to be accepted. She just needed someone to care. And now, years later, she still sleeps on the welcome mat at the church door. Because someone made space. And someone welcomed her.

05-without-a-place-not-without-a-shepher.book_title: Without a Place, Not Without a Shepherd

segments.v: We often talk about “finding God.” As if we’ve been out searching for Him. But the truth is — we were the ones behind the dumpster. And He was the one who came looking.
The Son of Man came to seek and to save the lost.

A man at a shelter once said, “I didn’t walk into that church looking for anything. I just wanted to warm up. But now I know — it was God who brought me there. He found me long before I knew I was missing.”

05-without-a-place-not-without-a-shepher.book_title: Without a Place, Not Without a Shepherd

segments.v: Jesus told a story about a shepherd. One sheep had wandered away. Just one. But the shepherd didn’t say, “Ninety-nine is good enough.” He left the others and went after the one that was lost.
If a man has a hundred sheep and one of them gets lost, what will he do? Won’t he leave the ninety-nine others in the wilderness and go to search for the one that is lost until he finds it?
Not if he finds it. Until. Because that’s who God is. He does not stop searching.

05-without-a-place-not-without-a-shepher.book_title: Without a Place, Not Without a Shepherd

segments.v:
The house of the Lord is not a place you visit. It’s a place where you are invited to stay, forever. Not because you earned it. Not because you’ve fixed everything about yourself and your life. But because the Shepherd lives there — and He wants you with Him.

A guests at a shelter once said, “I’ve lived under porches and overpasses. But the first time someone remembered my name two weeks in a row… that’s when I felt like I’d come home.”

05-without-a-place-not-without-a-shepher.book_title: Without a Place, Not Without a Shepherd

segments.v:
We’ve told stories about good but hurting dogs. But they were never the main character. They were there to remind us of something greater — of Someone greater. Because if a dog can wait at the door for someone who might never return, how much more does God wait for us? If a stray can offer the last crust to a friend, how much more does the Shepherd give?

If a nameless dog with no collar can be welcomed with a bowl of water, how much more are you seen? Known? Loved?

05-without-a-place-not-without-a-shepher.book_title: Without a Place, Not Without a Shepherd

segments.v:
But Charlie, Scout, and Baxter show us a different model. They’re not friends because they’re identical. They’re friends because they’ve chosen to walk through life together despite their differences. When one of them wants to sniff around the pond while another wants to chase tennis balls, they figure it out. They accommodate one another without losing themselves.

Real friendship isn’t about finding our clone. It’s about finding people who will walk alongside us, who will wait when we need to process something, who will celebrate our victories and stick around during our struggles. It’s about being that kind of friend to others, too.
As iron sharpens iron, so one person sharpens another.

Notice that this verse doesn’t say "as iron finds identical iron." Iron sharpens iron through friction, through difference, through the process of two distinct pieces helping each other become better. Good friends challenge us, support us, and help us grow in ways we never could alone.

Jesus modeled this perfectly. His closest friends were fishermen, tax collectors, political fanatics, and those who doubted. They disagreed about things. They had different personalities and backgrounds. But they walked together, learned together, and became better people because of their friendship.

In our friend group, we might be the Charlie (steady and calming), the Scout (energetic and driven), or the Baxter (the unique connector). That’s perfect. God uses different types of people to accomplish different things. The question isn’t whether we’re all the same; it’s whether we’re committed to walking together.

We need to be deliberate about good friendship. Just like the dog pack actively looks for one another, we need to be proactive about building and maintaining friendships. That might mean texting first, suggesting hangouts, remembering what’s important to our friends, or simply showing up consistently.

It also requires grace. Our friends will sometimes be in different moods, have different needs, or want to “sniff around the pond” when we want to keep moving. Learning to allow one another to be themselves without losing ourselves is one of the most valuable skills we’ll ever develop.

Look around. Who are the people God has placed in our lives to walk alongside? How can we be a better friend to them? And remember: the best friendships aren’t about being perfect together; they’re about choosing to stick together while we all become who God created us to be.

Today, Pepper is a different dog. She still has some sensitive moments, but she’s learned that not every hand will hurt her, not every voice will yell at her, not every change means danger. She’s discovered that

24 being vulnerable enough to trust actually leads to better things than self-protection ever could.

Pepper’s story hits close to home for many of us. Maybe we’ve been hurt by someone we trusted: a friend who betrayed a secret, a parent who let us down, a teacher who was unfair, a romantic relationship that ended badly. Maybe we’ve learned that putting ourselves out there can lead to pain, so it seems safer to keep our guard up.

When we learn to trust God completely, it actually becomes easier to trust people appropriately. We can take healthy risks in relationships because our security doesn’t depend on other people being perfect. We can be vulnerable because we know that even if someone hurts us, God will still be there to help us heal and grow.

Trusting God doesn’t guarantee that people will never disappoint us. But it does guarantee that disappointment isn’t the end of the story. Just like the shelter staff helped Pepper learn to trust again, God can heal our ability to connect with others even after we’ve been hurt.

This process takes time. Pepper didn’t transform overnight, and neither will we. There will be setbacks, moments when old fears resurface, times when we retreat back into self-protection mode. That’s normal and okay. Healing isn’t linear.

But here’s what Pepper discovered and what we can discover too: the risk of trusting is worth it. The alternative, a life of constant suspicion and emotional isolation, isn’t really protection. It’s just a different kind of prison.

Start small. Look for people who have proven themselves trustworthy in little things. Practice being vulnerable in safe relationships. Ask God to heal the places where we’ve been hurt and to help us figure out who deserves our trust.

Most importantly, remember that our ability to trust and love again isn’t just about our own healing; it’s about our capacity to be part of God’s work in the world. Just like Pepper now helps other nervous rescue dogs learn to trust, our healed heart can become a safe place for others who are learning to trust again too.

Maybe we’ve noticed this in ourselves or others. The straight-A student who can’t admit when they don’t understand something. The athlete who won’t try a new sport because they might not immediately excel. The person who’s so concerned with looking cool that they miss out on activities that might actually be fun. The social media presence that’s so carefully curated that it doesn’t reflect real life at all.

When we’re constantly performing or protecting our image, we miss out on the very experiences that make life meaningful. We become like Princess: technically impressive but somehow less alive than we could be.

When we’re dealing with the aftermath of our big mistakes, it feels like everything reliable in our life

15 disappears. Friends distance themselves. Family members lose trust. Opportunities vanish. The future we planned crumbles. We feel abandoned by everyone who once mattered to us.

But in the middle of all this loss and change, God’s faithfulness remains absolutely constant. His love doesn’t depend on our performance. His provision doesn’t fluctuate with our failures. His care for us isn’t conditional on our track record.

God’s daily provision works the same way. His love for us isn’t performance-based. His care isn’t conditional on our success in rebuilding our lives. His faithfulness doesn’t waver when we have setbacks or when healing takes longer than expected.

The people in our lives may come and go. Some relationships may never recover from our mistakes. But God’s commitment to us is like that 7:15 a.m. feeding schedule: absolutely reliable, completely consistent, never dependent on external circumstances.

When relationships feel unstable and your future feels uncertain, remind yourself: the feeding schedule hasn’t changed. God’s commitment to caring for you is not dependent on other people’s commitment to forgiving you. God’s faithfulness can be your constant, too. People may leave, but His love keeps showing up at 7:15 every morning.

We all have a kennel card that tells our story. Divorced. Fired. Bankrupted. Relapsed. Failed. The list of our returns and mistakes feels like a permanent record that disqualifies us from being chosen by anyone worthwhile.

When people get to know us, we brace for the moment they discover our history. We watch their faces change when they learn about our bankruptcy, our addiction, our infidelity, our arrest record. We’ve been returned before, and we expect it to happen again.

When you’re tempted to settle for relationships with people who will accept you “despite” your past, hold out for people who love you “because of” your whole story, including the broken parts. Someone is looking for you. Someone who will read your whole story and say, “That’s exactly the person I want.”

If you’re picking up this book, perhaps you know what it’s like to carry expectations that feel heavier than they should. Maybe you’ve sat through family gatherings where your achievements are the primary topic of conversation. Maybe you’ve

8 wondered whether people see you or just your family name. Maybe you’ve felt guilty for wanting something different than what’s been planned for you.

Or perhaps you’re a parent, grandparent, or mentor beginning to realize the weight your hopes might place on the young people you love. Maybe you’re starting to question whether the traditions you’ve held sacred are serving or suffocating the next generation.

The boy understood something then. The Lab had been isolated not because of anything wrong with him, but because of the family’s circumstances. Other dogs at the park did not care about security concerns or business deals or news coverage. They just wanted to play. And in that simple acceptance, the Lab had found what he needed. To be seen as what he really was: a friendly dog who loved to run and wrestle and share tennis balls.

Sometimes being truly known means stepping outside the protected spaces. It means risking genuine connection instead of staying safe behind windows. It means letting others see you for what you are, not what your family name suggests you should be.

The daughter looked up from her notes.

The teacher continued, “The other villagers saw her only through the lens of her relationships, her failures, her status as a Samaritan. But Jesus saw her as a complete person worthy of the deepest spiritual truths. He revealed His identity as the Messiah to her before anyone else. To religious leaders? No. To crowds? No. To one invisible woman at a well.”

The daughter’s pen stopped. She gripped it tighter as tears blurred her vision.

The father added quietly, “In the Bible, God says He knows us by name. Not by who our parents are or what they do. By our own names, each person individually. He doesn’t see you as ‘our daughter who should follow our path.’ He sees you. Your own person. Your own calling. We forgot to see you that way.”

“There’s this story,” the daughter said, “about Jesus and a woman at a well. Everyone knew about her, but Jesus was the first person to really see her. To talk to her about real things. To treat her as a whole person worthy of truth.” She looked at her parents. “I need you to see me that way. As a whole person.”

The daughter thought about this as she worked. We can bring everything to Jesus. What we really want to do with our lives. Our disappointments in the people who love us but don’t seem to know the real us. The pressure from parents or from others to go one way, when we desperately want to go a different way. Our way. He sees it all. He knows it all. And He is always with us. He has been. He is now. And He always will be.

She thought about that. “So you’re saying I should just… stay? Even when my own people don’t understand?”

“I’m saying you have to decide what you’re staying for. And who you’re staying with.” He paused. “Jesus said something once that always stuck with me. He said whoever loves father or mother more than Him isn’t worthy of Him. Harsh words. But honest ones.”

“That’s what scares me. Choosing Jesus feels like choosing against my people.”

“Does it? Or does it feel like choosing something bigger than any one group of people?” Her father looked up at the stars. “Our ancestors watched these same stars. They knew there was something greater than themselves. I think Jesus is the answer to what they were looking for.”

“I feel like I’m supposed to choose,” she said quietly. “My people or my faith. Like I can’t have both.”

“Who told you that?”

“Everyone. The students in the group say Christianity destroyed us. And sometimes at church, people act like our traditions are all just paganism to be left behind.”

Her father was quiet for a long moment. “Do you remember what Numu means?”

“The people.”

“The people. That’s who we are. And Jesus? He came for all people. He was born under Roman occupation. He knew what it was like to have His people oppressed.”

A hawk circled overhead. Pia had moved the sheep to the water and sat watching them. His ears were up but his body was relaxed.

“So what do I do when they say I’m betraying my people by following Jesus?”

“You love them anyway. You stay connected anyway. You trust that Jesus is worth following even when your own community doesn’t understand.”

She went back to school. She kept attending the Indigenous student group meetings, even when the tension grew. When she mentioned praying about an issue, some of the others rolled their eyes. When she declined to participate in a ceremony

Nods of solidarity around the room.

“And that was evil,” she continued. “Every bit of it. But it wasn’t Jesus. It was people using His name for something He never taught. My Jesus spoke Aramaic, not English. He kept Jewish customs, not European ones. He said let the little children come to Him, not beat them into submission.”

She took a breath. “I follow Jesus because He transcends all cultures. He’s not a white god or a brown god. He’s the God who made everyone. And following Him has taught me that my worth isn’t in my bloodline or my grades or even my heritage. It’s in being loved by the Creator Himself.”

“You’re still choosing their religion,” the president said.

“I’m choosing Jesus. There’s a difference.” She gathered her things. “I love our people. I love our languages, our songs, our stories. But I love Jesus more. And that’s not betrayal. That’s freedom.”

She walked out. Her hands were shaking, but her spirit was calm.

Three months later, her father visited for Thanksgiving. They walked across the university campus together. Pia trotted beside them. Students stopped to admire him, but he paid them no attention. He passed other dogs near the park without reacting.

Pastor Kim thought about God’s love. God doesn’t love His people because they’re impressive or talented or useful in ministry programs. He loves them because He chooses to love them. His love isn’t based on what they accomplish. It’s based on His character.

Just like Charlie waiting at the door, God’s love is patient and constant. He doesn’t give up on His people when they’re late to church or struggling with faith. He doesn’t stop loving them when they mess up or fail to serve. He keeps showing up, keeps waiting, keeps loving them with the kind of faithful heart that never lets go.

This weekly drama is more than dog behavior. Like Penny, we have our own habit of wandering off from the safe boundaries of love. We go after interesting things down roads that lead away from home. We explore places that promise excitement but ultimately leave us unsafe, muddy, and tired.

And like Penny, at the end of our adventure we find ourselves standing on the porch, hoping to find forgiveness and acceptance. The truth is that God’s door will always open. He always says, “There you are. I’ve been waiting for you.”

The Bible tells us about a man who struggled with names and identity his entire life. Jacob’s name meant “deceiver,” and he lived up to it for years. He manipulated his brother, tricked his father, and spent decades running from the consequences of his choices. But one night, after wrestling with God until dawn, he received a new name: Israel, which means “God fights for you.”

The interesting thing is that even after receiving his new name, Jacob was still called by his old name throughout Scripture. But something had changed. The name didn’t define him anymore. What mattered wasn’t what people called him, but who he belonged to and who was fighting for him.

But when his name was called, even from way across the field, he’d pause. Sometimes he’d hesitate. Sometimes he’d give that look. Really? Right now? But then, every time, he’d come running.

That’s the kind of heart we want to have with God. One that hears His voice, even when we’ve wandered far. A heart that turns back, even if it takes a second.

The truth is, we have had seasons where we didn’t respond right away. Where we ignored the gentle nudge. Where we told ourselves we’d come back later. But the longer we waited, the harder it felt to return. Not because God had changed his mind or moved away; He is always waiting for us to return, with arms wide open. Always.

God never stops calling. Even when we mess up. Even when we shut ourselves in. Even when we go too far. He calls us by name. Not with anger, but with kindness. Not to shame us, but to bring us back to Him.

Jesus once told a story about a lost sheep. It wandered off. And instead of yelling at it or waiting for it to return on its own, the shepherd went after it. When he found it, he didn’t scold it. With relief, thankfulness, and gladness, he brought it home.

That’s what God does. He calls us. And when we come, even with our heads down and feeling sorry, He gives us the biggest, warmest bear hug. He does not guilt trip us. He does not lecture us. He just embraces us with a love we will not fully understand this side of heaven.

Dogs instinctively know something about that. Probably. They might wander. But when they hear their name, they come running.

Let’s be like that. Because no matter how far we’ve gone, wouldn’t we rather be near Him than anywhere else?

They don’t carry their regrets. They don’t replay it. They leave the past behind. And they keep walking.

Don’t we all wish we were more like that?

We carry things. Sometimes more than we should. Old regrets. Words we said and wish we hadn’t. Moments we replay over and over, trying to fix what’s already finished. But the past doesn’t change, no matter how long we carry it with us. And the more we stare at it, the harder it is to see what’s ahead.

There’s a verse in the Bible that says, “Forget the former things. Do not dwell on the past. See, I am doing a new thing.”

Dwell. That word means to live in something. To stay there. And we don’t want to live in old mistakes anymore. We want to live in what God is doing now.

Let’s learn how to let go. Not to forget completely, but to stop holding on so tight. To trust that God’s mercy and grace really does mean we are forgiven and loved. And that even when we mess up, God doesn’t walk away. He still calls us with open arms.

Dogs live in the moment. They mess up, but then after a little while they look for what’s next. They keep wagging. They keep walking.

Leave the past with the One who can carry it. Stop staring backward. Look forward. Walk ahead. We are forgiven. We have been unchained. We are loved. And we are not alone.

Jesus told a story about a son who ran away. He spent everything he had. He made a mess of his life. And when he finally decided to go back home, tired, scared, and not sure what to expect, his father ran out to meet him.

His father didn’t yell at him. He wasn’t mad at him. He didn’t punish him. He threw his arms around him and said, “Welcome home.”

That’s what God is like.

No matter how far we’ve gone, He still wants us home. And not just someday. He wants us home right now. He wants to give us His peace, now. He gives us a deep sense that we are not lost anymore.

Home isn’t always a place. But it’s always a Person. It’s where love meets us at the door, of a place, of our hearts, and says, “You’re safe here.”

And when this life is done , when all the noise fades and the walking is over, He will meet us again. And even if we have a long list of things we got wrong, He will remind us that He forgave us, for every item on that list and a whole bunch more that we forgot about. He will take us in with open arms and a smile that says, “Welcome. You made it. I love you.”

But the moment you roll in the mud, you lose more than your position — you lose your integrity, your witness.

The apostle Paul once wrote, “Do not be overcome by evil, but overcome evil with good.” (Romans 12:21) That’s not a slogan. That’s a command. The world already has enough mud. What it needs are people willing to stay clean — even when it’s messy.

That doesn’t mean you need to always stay quiet. It doesn’t mean you ignore injustice. It means you answer with truth — not with tricks.

03-one-voice-one-leash-one-lord-023.book_title: One Voice, One Leash, One Lord

segments.v:
Jesus could’ve out-argued every Pharisee. Easily. But He never stooped to their level. He didn’t roll in their mud. He humbly and gently stood firm in the light of truth.

When the world plays dirty, shine brighter. When politics gets ugly, speak with grace. You don’t have to match the mess to make a difference.

You just have to stay clean — even if you’re the only one who does.

03-one-voice-one-leash-one-lord-024.book_title: One Voice, One Leash, One Lord

segments.v:
“I had a professor who never let us forget our mistakes,” the new vet said. “He said it would make us better vets.”

“Did it?”

“No. It just made us afraid to try.”

The tech nodded. “I’ve been doing this for years. You want to know the secret to lasting in this field?”

The new vet looked up.

“Learn from your mistakes, then let them go. Like Honey with that cat. She’ll probably never chase another cat in the clinic. But she’s not going to spend the rest of her life feeling guilty about it either.”

The new vet found Honey in the recovery room, lying beside the waking patient.

“You know what I learned?” she told the tech later. “That first surgery that went wrong? It taught me what to watch for. If I hadn’t made that mistake, I might not have caught the problem today.”

The tech smiled. “Mistakes are just experience in disguise.”

Months passed. The new vet grew more confident. She still made mistakes, as everyone did, but she’d learned to acknowledge them, learn from them, and move forward.

The clinic’s philosophy had become clear over time: excellence wasn’t about perfection. It was about learning, growing, and not letting fear of failure prevent them from trying.

Honey demonstrated this daily. She’d learned not to chase cats in the clinic, but she still played with them at home. She’d learned to avoid the vaccine fridge, but still checked the break room for crumbs. Each mistake had taught her something without diminishing her spirit.

“Grace,” the tech said one day, watching Honey comfort a scared puppy getting its first shots. “That’s what she has. Grace to forgive herself and others.”

That’s what stuck with everyone who worked there. What mattered was not the mistakes, but the recovery, not the failures, but the grace to try again.

Scripture says we all fall short, but grace covers our shortcomings. Honey lived that truth every day, teaching a clinic full of perfectionists that healing, whether for animals or humans, began with forgiving ourselves.

The clinic kept her collar hanging in the break room after she passed. Below it, a small sign: “Mistakes happen. Grace happens more.”
You knit me together in my mother’s womb… I am fearfully and wonderfully made.

We are not accidents. We are not our bruises. We are not what we’ve lost. We are not less human because we hurt.

That black dog didn’t wag his tail for almost a week. He just watched. Waited. Listened. But the first time someone said, “You’re safe here,” something seemed to have changed. He curled up that night, not just because he was tired, but because he finally believed — he belonged.

05-without-a-place-not-without-a-shepher.book_title: Without a Place, Not Without a Shepherd

segments.v: Maybe you didn’t come looking for Him today. Maybe you’ve spent years feeling like you’re beyond reach, beyond grace, beyond the kind of rescue songs are written about. But the Shepherd sees where you are. And He’s already walking your way.
I will answer them before they even call to me. While they are still talking about their needs, I will go ahead and answer their prayers!

That dog behind the dumpster never asked to be seen. He didn’t have the courage. He didn’t even know how to ask. But someone saw him anyway. Someone leaned down, came down to where he was. And that’s how healing began. Not because he went looking. But because love found him first.

05-without-a-place-not-without-a-shepher.book_title: Without a Place, Not Without a Shepherd

segments.v:
Speak up when someone is being bullied, even if it makes us unpopular. Say no to something you know is wrong, even when everyone else is saying yes. Be honest about our faith, even in environments where that’s not cool.

Sometimes courage is big and obvious, like Bear’s protective stance. But often it’s smaller and quieter: having a difficult conversation with a friend, admitting when you’ve made a mistake, or choosing to do the right thing even when no one is watching.

This might mean defending someone who’s being excluded or mocked, refusing to participate in gossip, even when it’s juicy, being honest about our struggles instead of pretending to be perfect, standing up for our convictions even when it costs us socially, asking for help when we’re in over our heads, apologizing when we’ve hurt someone.

Courage often involves protecting something valuable: relationships, integrity, truth, justice, or other people’s wellbeing. Like Bear, we need to figure out when courage is called for and when it’s not. Not every disagreement requires a confrontation. Not every wrong needs to be our personal crusade. Wisdom helps us pick our battles.

Murphy learned from his mistake, but he didn’t let shame make him feel like he was a bad dog.

Murphy’s story perfectly captures something we all struggle with: what to do when we mess up badly. And if you’re a teenager, mistakes feel extra painful because everything seems really important. A bad grade? Future ruined. A friendship conflict feels like the end of the world. A poor choice feels like it defines us forever.

But Murphy shows us a healthier way to handle failure. He didn’t minimize what he did wrong. His guilt was real and appropriate. But he also didn’t let that guilt paralyze him or convince him that he was too bad to change. He faced what happened, learned from the experience, and moved forward.

The key difference between guilt and shame is this: guilt says, “I did something bad,” while shame says, “I

48 am bad.” Guilt can lead to positive change. Shame just leads to hiding and hopelessness.
If we confess our sins, He is faithful and just and will forgive us our sins and purify us from all unrighteousness.

This verse contains one of the most liberating truths in all of Scripture: forgiveness is available, complete, and immediate for anyone who genuinely asks for it. We don’t have to earn it, work for it, or prove we deserve it. We just have to honestly admit what we did wrong and receive what God freely offers.

But forgiveness doesn’t stop with God. Jesus taught us to pray, “Forgive us our debts, as we also have forgiven our debtors” (Matthew 6:12). Part of receiving forgiveness is learning to forgive others and to forgive ourselves.

This is particularly challenging for teenagers, who often struggle with perfectionism and self-criticism. We might find it easier to forgive friends for their mistakes than to forgive ourselves for ours. But

49 holding onto guilt after God has forgiven us isn’t humility; it’s pride. It’s as if we are saying that our standards are higher than God’s.

Admit the damage honestly. Don’t minimize, excuse, or blame others. Take responsibility for our choices and their consequences. Make things right where possible. Apologize sincerely. Replace what can be replaced. Learn what needs to be learned. Accept forgiveness gracefully. Whether it comes from God, others, or ourselves, receive it as the gift it is. Don’t keep punishing ourselves after we’ve already made it right.

Learn and move forward. Let the experience teach you something valuable, but don’t let it define you. Extend the same grace to others. When friends mess up, remember how good it felt to be forgiven instead of criticized.

Murphy didn’t stay on the couch that he partially destroyed feeling sorry for himself. He got back to the business of being a good dog, with better understanding for how to handle his anxiety. Like Murphy, our mistakes don’t disqualify us from our purpose; they can actually equip us to help others who are struggling with similar issues.

Everyone messes up. Everyone needs forgiveness. Everyone deserves a chance to start over. The question isn’t whether we’ll make mistakes; it’s whether we’ll let those mistakes teach us about the grace of God and the chance to start again.

Remember: we are not the worst thing we’ve ever done. We are beloved children of God. We are learning how to live wisely, and sometimes that learning involves some pretty spectacular failures. That’s not a bug in the system; it’s a feature. Even couch-eating mistakes can become part of our testimony about God’s grace.

Dealing with addiction that has destroyed relationships? Betrayed someone who loved us? Hurt our children through our poor decisions?

Feel torn apart…? Like being ripped from the life we once knew, like we have too much baggage, like our mistakes have made us unlovable.

We want you to know something before you read these twelve lessons: You are still loved.

Not because our mistakes don’t matter. They do. Not because consequences aren’t real. They are. Not because we don’t have work to do. We do.

But because being torn doesn’t make us worthless. Because people can make terrible choices and still be people who need love, forgiveness, and second chances. Because love doesn’t depend on having a perfect, untorn life.

The dogs in this book taught us that being in an impossibly difficult place doesn’t erase our value. It just means we are in need of care, patience, and knowing that Someone does see our heart instead of just our history. We believe God sees us that way.

These are not complicated theology lessons. These are simple stories about hope finding dogs in challenging situations. Love founding their torn-up lives. People who wanted them exactly as they were and taking them home.

If that sounds like something you need to hear, this book is for you. We are all torn in some way. But we are still loved by God. And that makes all the difference.

A Note About These Stories

The dogs and people in this book are based on real dogs and real people. We have observed these behaviors, situations, and outcomes at shelters and in life. However, all names have been changed to protect privacy. Some details have been combined or adjusted to better illustrate the lessons, but every story is grounded in actual experiences we have witnessed.

“This dog isn’t aggressive,” she tells the Matthews family. “He had a resource-guarding response to an unexpected situation. With proper management and some retraining, he can absolutely remain a safe family pet.”

“Everyone makes mistakes,” she adds. “Even good dogs. That doesn’t erase who they really are.”

We’ve all had our Kingsman moment. That one terrible decision, that moment when fear or anger or weakness took over and we did something that horrified even ourselves. Maybe we lost our temper and said unforgivable words. Maybe we made a choice that destroyed a marriage. Maybe we betrayed someone’s trust or hurt someone we love.

Now we carry our own warning label. Every time we meet someone new, we wonder if they know. Every time we look in the mirror, we see that red flag. We’ve convinced ourselves that one terrible moment defines our entire worth.

But God doesn’t see us that way.
The Lord does not look at the things people look at. People look at the outward appearance, but the Lord looks at the heart.

God spoke these words when everyone was judging by external appearances, by past performance, by what seemed obvious on the surface. But God sees deeper. He sees our heart, not just the moment when it failed, but what it’s truly made of.

We live in a world that loves to label people by their worst moment. One mistake becomes our permanent identity. One failure defines our future. One bad decision erases years of good character.

But God’s perspective is different. He sees Kingsman as a good dog who had a scared moment, not a dangerous dog who occasionally acts nice. He sees us as His beloved children who stumbled, not as failures who occasionally do something right.

Kingsman’s worth wasn’t determined by that one afternoon in the yard. Three years of gentleness, patience, and love couldn’t be erased by one moment

The behaviorist helped him see his true character. And that’s exactly how God sees us.

Our identity isn’t our worst decision. Our worth isn’t our biggest failure. Our future isn’t imprisoned by our past. We are who God says we are: loved, valuable, worthy of second chances.

The warning label feels permanent, but it’s not. Reputations can be rebuilt. Hearts can heal. Good dogs who make mistakes are still good dogs. They just need proper support and people who see their heart.

God is like that experienced behaviorist. He sees our whole history, not just our worst moment. He looks at our heart and says, “That’s my child who had a bad moment. That doesn’t erase who they really are.” Our warning label doesn’t have to be permanent. We’re still worthy of love. We’re still valuable to God.
But God doesn’t choose people based on clean records.

But God chose the foolish things of the world to shame the wise; God chose the weak things of the world to shame the strong. God chose the lowly things of this world and the despised things, and the things that are not, to nullify the things that are.

Paul wrote this to people who felt unqualified for God’s love. They looked around at successful, respectable, religious people and assumed God preferred that type. They read their own kennel cards and assumed they were damaged goods.

But Paul explains that God’s selection process is backwards from human logic. He doesn’t choose people in spite of their failures - He often chooses them because of their failures. Broken people understand grace in ways that successful people never do.

The word “chose” here is active and intentional. God didn’t settle for us because the good people weren’t available. He specifically selected us, knowing our full history, because He saw something He wanted.

The Martinez family didn’t choose Luna despite her past. They chose her because they understood what her past meant. Her escape attempts weren’t rebellion; they were evidence of how deeply she loves and how desperately she fears abandonment. Her “failures” revealed her heart.

God reads our whole story the same way. Our mistakes don’t disqualify us from His love. They qualify us to understand His grace. Our failures don’t make us undesirable. They make us precisely the kind of people He’s looking for.

When God looks at our kennel card, He doesn’t see damaged goods. He sees someone who will understand mercy because they’ve needed it. He sees someone who will love deeply because they know what it costs. He sees someone whose story of redemption will encourage others who feel unredeemable.

Later, the shelter manager tells the story to anyone who’ll listen. “Diesel saved lives today. He found his voice again when it really mattered.”

After the incident that ended his police career, Diesel learned that his judgment could be wrong, that his voice could cause problems, that maybe it was safer to

31 let others make the decisions. Staying quiet meant avoiding the risk of being wrong again.

Even when he saw the flooding, Diesel was tempted to wait for someone else to notice, someone else to take responsibility, someone else to risk being wrong. It would have been easier to stay silent and let others decide whether this was worth responding to. But silence would have cost lives.

When we make a big mistake that damages our credibility, when we’re wrong about something important, when our judgment fails us, when our voice leads to harm, we often overcorrect by going completely silent. We stop offering opinions, stop taking leadership, stop speaking up even when we see problems. But sometimes our voice is exactly what someone else needs to survive.

“Speak up for those who cannot speak for themselves, for the rights of all who are destitute. Speak up and judge fairly; defend the rights of the poor and needy.”

— Proverbs 31:8-9

These words were written as advice for leaders, but they apply to anyone who has a voice and sees injustice. Notice that the command isn’t to speak up for yourself, but for those who cannot speak for themselves. Our voice matters most when it’s used to protect others, not defend ourselves.

The passage doesn’t say “speak up when you’re confident you won’t make mistakes” or “speak up when your credibility is perfect.” It says speak up for those who need an advocate, period. Sometimes being wrong about smaller things prepares us to be right about bigger things.

Diesel’s past mistake made him doubt his judgment about everything. But the flooding wasn’t about his career or his confidence; it was about Sophie standing helpless in rising water. When he focused on someone else’s need instead of his own fear of being wrong again, he found his voice.

We may have lost credibility in some areas of our life due to past failures. People might not trust our judgment about certain topics anymore. We might not trust our own judgment. But that doesn’t mean our voice has no value.

There are people who need someone to speak up for them: people facing injustice, people being taken advantage of, people who can’t advocate for themselves. Our past mistakes don’t disqualify us from defending others; they might actually qualify us to recognize when someone needs help.

Finding your voice again isn’t about restoring your reputation; it’s about being useful to others. Your voice might be exactly what someone needs to hear today. Don’t let fear of being wrong again keep you silent when someone is standing in rising water.

But both chose to try again.

Some of us make our biggest mistakes later in life, when it feels like we should have known better. The 45-year-old who destroys a 20-year marriage. The 55-year-old who makes a career-ending ethical lapse. The 35-year-old parent whose addiction costs them custody of their children.

When we fail after we’re supposed to be mature and wise, it feels like we’ve missed our chance for redemption. Starting over seems impossible when we’re no longer young, when our mistakes have gray hair, when everyone expects us to have figured out life by now.
But God specializes in second chapters.
Therefore, if anyone is in Christ, The new creation has come: The old has gone, the new is here!

Paul wrote this to people who thought their past disqualified them from God’s love. Some were former criminals, some were former addicts, some were former religious zealots who had persecuted Christians. Many were middle-aged or older when they encountered Jesus.

The word “new” doesn’t mean young. It means fresh, different, unprecedented. God doesn’t make us younger; He makes us newer. Our chronological age doesn’t limit His ability to give us a completely fresh start.

Caesar, at nine years old, was just as capable of becoming a “new creation” as a puppy would be. Eleanor, at 67, was just as ready for a new beginning as someone half her age. Age doesn’t disqualify us from transformation.

Caesar’s second chapter with Eleanor was different from his first life, but it was just as meaningful. He learned new routines, new ways of showing affection, new reasons to wag his tail. His arthritis didn’t disappear, but it didn’t prevent him from loving and being loved.

Our second chapter after a major failure won’t be identical to our first chapter, but it can be just as significant. We’ll carry some scars, some limitations, some consequences from our mistakes. But those don’t prevent us from building something new and meaningful.

Caesar didn’t become a puppy again. He became a wiser, gentler version of himself. Our redemption doesn’t erase our history; it transforms our future.

Wisdom gained through mistakes can make our second chapter richer than our first. Our failures can give us empathy and insight that make our new beginning more meaningful. Love after loss is still love. Starting over after many years is still starting over. Second chapters can be just as precious as first ones.

When we’ve done something that puts us in the “dangerous” category - when we’ve hurt someone, broken trust, or made choices that scare people. It’s easy to believe we’ve lost our right to kindness. We assume we don’t deserve the same grace that others receive.

We might even stop expecting good things. After enough doors close, enough people distance themselves, enough opportunities disappear, we start believing that mercy has limits and we’ve exceeded ours. But God’s grace doesn’t have a “dangerous dog” exception.
But God demonstrates his own love for us in this: While we were still sinners, Christ died for us.

Paul wrote this to people who thought they had to clean up their act before God would love them. They assumed that grace was only for “good people” who

52 made minor mistakes, not for “dangerous people” who’d caused serious harm.

But God’s love isn’t based on our safety rating. Christ died for us while we were still sinners. Not after we proved we’d changed, not after we demonstrated we were safe, not after we earned the right to be loved. While we were still dangerous.

The word ‘demonstrates’ means to prove or show clearly. God wanted to make absolutely sure we understood that His love doesn’t have exceptions for people with warning labels.

Bruno’s first walk didn’t erase his bite history or instantly make him safe. But it proved that the policy of care applied to him too, that isolation wasn’t his permanent sentence, that someone was willing to follow the rules of compassion even when it was difficult.

God’s daily mercies work the same way. They don’t instantly erase our past or immediately resolve all consequences of our actions. But they prove that His policy of love applies to us too, that isolation from His care isn’t our permanent sentence, that He’s willing to extend grace even when we seem dangerous.

Every morning Bruno got a walk was a reminder that he was still worth the effort, still deserving of basic dignity, still capable of rehabilitation. Every mercy God shows us is the same reminder.

Practice gratitude for basic dignities you might be taking for granted. Bruno was amazed by a simple walk that other dogs considered routine. What ordinary mercies in your life deserve more appreciation?

Receiving grace when you don’t deserve it is exactly how grace works. If you deserved it, it would be payment, not grace. The fact that you feel unworthy is actually evidence that what you’re receiving is genuine mercy.

After we’ve done significant work to rebuild our life following a major mistake, we face Rex’s choice. Do we focus entirely on protecting our own progress, or do we use our experience to help others who are earlier in the process?

There’s wisdom in being protective of our recovery. But there’s also profound purpose in using our story of transformation to offer hope to people who are where we used to be.

“Praise be to the God and Father of our Lord Jesus Christ, the Father of compassion and the God of all comfort, who comforts us in all our troubles, so that we can comfort those in any trouble with the comfort we ourselves receive from God.”

— 2 Corinthians 1:3-4

Paul wrote this to people who had experienced significant hardship and healing. He explained that God’s comfort isn’t just for our personal relief. It’s meant to flow through us to others who need the same healing we’ve received.

The phrase “so that we can” indicates purpose. God doesn’t allow us to go through difficult experiences just for our own growth. He intends for our healing to become a resource for others who are struggling with similar issues.

Notice that Paul doesn’t say we should help others after we’re completely healed. He says we should comfort others with the comfort we ourselves are receiving. Rex was still in the shelter, still working on

64 his own issues, but that didn’t disqualify him from helping Spike.

Rex’s ability to help Spike came directly from his own experience of fear, aggression, and eventual healing. He couldn’t have taught what he hadn’t learned, couldn’t have offered hope he hadn’t experienced, couldn’t have demonstrated transformation he hadn’t undergone.

Our greatest qualifications for helping others often come from our greatest failures and recoveries. The person who’s overcome addiction understands recovery in ways that textbooks never could. The person who’s rebuilt a marriage after infidelity knows what forgiveness actually requires. The person who’s restored their relationship with their children after major mistakes understands both the damage and the possibility.

Our scars become our credentials for ministry to others with similar wounds. Share our story carefully and appropriately. Model better ways of responding to stress and fear. Sometimes our example speaks louder than our advice.

Notice what Jesus didn’t say. He didn’t say, “if you qualify.” He didn’t say, “if you clean up your mistakes.” He didn’t say, “if you prove you're worthy.” He said, “I will come back and take you to be with me.” The promise isn’t based on our performance. It’s guaranteed by His character.

Jesus understands what it feels like to be separated from the people and place we love most. He understands waiting for restoration that sometimes feels like it will never come. And He promises that our going home day is not just possible. It’s certain.

Charlie’s adoption day happened for a reason. Someone had been preparing for him long before he knew they existed. The Williams family had been looking for exactly the kind of dog Charlie was. This included his history of abandonment and return. His past wasn’t something that disqualified him. It was part of what made him perfect for them.

God has been preparing for our homecoming since before we knew we needed one. Our history of mistakes and returns doesn’t disqualify us from His love. It’s part of what makes His grace so perfect for exactly who we are. We don’t have to earn our place in God’s family. We just need to be ready to say yes when He comes for us.

We believe God works the same way. Only deeper. Only truer. Only more completely than we can ever imagine.

If you’re reading this while carrying the weight of a terrible mistake, we hope these simple stories

72 reminded you of something important. We are not our worst moment. We are not forgotten. We are not too damaged to be loved. We are not beyond redemption.

Maybe we lost everything in a divorce that was our fault. Maybe we are carrying guilt from hurting our children through our choices. Maybe we destroyed a career through poor decisions. Maybe we betrayed someone who trusted us. Maybe we are struggling with addiction that has cost us relationships and respect.

Whatever brought you to feel ‘damaged,’ we want you to know: you are still loved. We, of course, are not perfect. Far from it. We have consequences to face. We have work to do. Lots of work. But we are still loved to our core. Still worthy of love. Still capable of change. Still valuable to God.

Kingsman learned that his worth wasn’t determined by one scared moment in the yard. Rico discovered that faithfulness shows up every day, no matter what. Luna found out that someone was looking for her whole story, not just the easy parts. Tank proved that real character shows up in challenging times, not easy ones.

Diesel found his voice again when someone else needed help. Ziggy learned that a limited view doesn’t mean limited possibilities. Caesar discovered it’s never too late for a second chapter. Rosie mastered the art of waiting well. Bruno experienced grace that has no exceptions. Bella received the most tender care when she was most broken. Rex used his healing to help others heal. And Charlie never stopped believing that his going-home day was real.

These are not just dog stories. They are our stories. The stories of anyone who has ever felt lost, broken, returned, or forgotten.

We have seen people love these dogs with tenderness; God loves us the same way, but unimaginably and infinitely more. People see potential in damaged animals; God sees potential in damaged people. Some good people never give up on good dogs who’ve had bad days; God never gives up on His people who’ve made mistakes.

Our doors will open. Our restoration day will come. Someone is preparing a place for us. Someone who knows our whole story and wants us anyway.

We’ve learned that dogs don’t read pedigree papers. They don’t care about bloodlines or breeding certificates. A shelter mutt will love you just as fiercely as a champion show dog. And perhaps more importantly, they teach us that worth isn’t determined by lineage.

In the same way, God doesn’t check our family tree before offering His love. He doesn’t measure our value by how well we fulfill family expectations or cultural traditions. His love isn’t conditional on whether we become what our parents hoped or what our heritage demands.

The boy thought about a story from the Bible. In the book of John, Jesus said something important about light and darkness. He said that people who do evil hide in darkness because they do not want their deeds exposed. But those who live by the truth come into the light. The passage was not saying that struggles or problems are evil. It was saying that hiding things in darkness gives them more power. Bringing them into the light is where healing begins.
