Their dog had taught them this lesson. When he tried to hide his fear alone in dark spaces, the anxiety grew worse. When the family brought the problem into the light, acknowledged it honestly, and worked on it together, healing became possible.

A few weeks later, the veterinarian’s friend visited again with her daughter. When thunder started, the dog walked to his safe room. The visiting girl watched and smiled. “He’s doing so much better,” she said. The boy nodded.

The boy watched him as their dog emerged from his safe room later that evening. He was moving calmly instead of frantically. Their willingness to be honest about their struggles was what made it

37 possible for him to start healing. The family’s openness about their struggles could help other families feel less alone with theirs.

Sometimes the bravest thing we can do is stop hiding. Sometimes the strongest families are not the ones who never struggle, but the ones who face their struggles honestly, together, in the light.

One evening, as the daughter worked on her graphic novel with Ghost sprawled across her entire bedroom floor, she thought about the woman at the well. After Jesus saw her, really saw her, He didn’t condemn her. He knew about the five husbands, the searching for love in all the wrong places. And He offered her living water, the fulfillment she’d been seeking all along. The woman was fully known, but she didn’t have to hide anymore. She was known. She was seen. And she was loved.

Create. Not repair. Not improve. Not try harder. Create. The way only God creates, from nothing, where there was nothing before.

David had written that psalm after his own betrayal. He had not asked God to help him fix what he had broken. He had asked God to make something entirely new, because he knew that what was broken could not be fixed by human hands. Not his. Not anyone’s.

The wife sat on the porch and felt the weight of that word. Create. She had been trying to rebuild trust with her own willpower, the way you rebuild a wall, brick by brick, through effort and discipline. But trust was not a wall. Trust was a living thing, and it had died, and no amount of effort can bring a dead thing back to life.

So whether you’re dealing with the pressure of high school, figuring out friendships, worrying about the future, or just trying to understand what it means to follow Jesus in daily life, we invite you to pause and consider what these good dogs might teach us about our good God.

After all, if the Creator of the universe can speak through a burning bush, He can certainly use a Golden Retriever to remind us about enthusiasm, or a rescue dog to teach us about trust, or a loyal mutt to show us what faithfulness looks like.

The daughter started crying again. “I don’t even believe in God anymore.”

The mother reached across the table. “Then we’ll believe for you until you find your way. You’re still our daughter. And you’re still God’s child.”

The father looked at the son. “And you don’t have to be perfect. You can be yourself. Make mistakes. Learn who you are. We’ll love you the same.”

That Sunday, the daughter stayed home from church. The parents didn’t make excuses. They simply said, “Pray for our family.”

“Do you still wonder?”

“Not really. I just watch him now. A dog who stays. A dog who works. A dog who is relaxed. He eats well. He sleeps through the night.”

Pia’s eyes were half-closed now. His breathing was slow.

“Maybe that’s faith,” she said. “Not knowing everything, but trusting anyway. Staying even when you hear the howling.”

Her father smiled. “That sounds about right.”

The evening light was turning golden over the campus. Soon her father would drive back to the reservation, back to the sheep and the creek and the sound of the wild pack at dusk.

God sees you. He knows you. He loves you. He loves you in your questions and in your certainty. His love does not depend on what you preserve or what you become. You are already enough. You always have been.

Pastor Martinez looked at Gida, who hadn’t moved from his post by the window. The dog’s ears were alert, his eyes bright, and his tail occasionally gave a hopeful wag when a car drove by. Gida didn’t check the time or complain about waiting. He simply believed that David would come home, and he was going to be ready when it happened.

“Doesn’t he get tired of waiting?” Pastor Martinez asked.

Mrs. Kim smiled softly. “I used to worry about that. I tried to get him to play with toys or go for walks, thinking he needed distractions from his waiting. But I realized something: Gida isn’t suffering while he waits. He’s hopeful.”

But Gida demonstrated a different kind of waiting; his waiting was one filled with expectation rather than frustration. Gida wasn’t angry that David hadn’t returned yet. He wasn’t checking his watch or giving up hope. He was simply maintaining his faithful vigil, believing that good things were worth waiting for.

The next Sunday, Pastor Martinez preached about hope that endures. He talked about how waiting on God isn’t passive resignation, but active faith.

It’s believing that God’s timing is perfect even when we can’t see the bigger picture.

“Sometimes we think that if God doesn’t answer our prayers quickly, He’s forgotten about us,” Pastor Martinez told his congregation. “But maybe His delays are part of His perfect plan. Maybe He’s preparing something better than we originally asked for.”

A few months later, Pastor Martinez received an unexpected phone call. A local business owner had heard about their church’s needs through a mutual friend. This man wanted to donate a piece of land, which was much larger and better located than anything the church could have afforded to buy. The building they could construct on this land would serve not just their current congregation, but could accommodate future growth and community outreach programs they had only dreamed about.

A few weeks later, Mrs. Kim called Pastor Martinez with excitement in her voice. David was coming home. His deployment had ended earlier than expected, and he would be arriving that weekend.

When David finally walked through the front door, Gida’s joy was explosive. His tail wagged so hard his whole body shook. He danced and spun in circles, as if all his patient waiting had finally paid off.

Pastor Martinez realized that Gida had taught him something important about faith. Hope isn’t about knowing when prayers will be answered. Hope is about believing they will be answered and staying faithful while you wait.

Just like Gida painted welcome signs with his wagging tail, believers can live with expectant hearts, trusting that God’s love never forgets and His timing is always perfect.

The church’s new building project began six months later, but Pastor Martinez had already received the greater gift - learning how to wait with hope and trust.

Why write about dogs? Because they seem to naturally do many of the things that we struggle with as humans. They wake up excited about ordinary days. They forgive quickly and completely. They’re loyal without keeping score. They trust deeply, rest without guilt, and love unconditionally. They don’t worry

5 about their image or compete for status. They just... live well.

As we’ve watched these good dogs over the years, we’ve begun to see something beautiful: they reflect the character of our good God in ways that both surprise and encourage us. Not because dogs are perfect; they’re not. But because they embody qualities that God values and wants to develop in us. Their enthusiasm mirrors God’s joy over each new day He gives us. Their faithfulness reflects His unchanging love. Their ability to forgive and trust again shows us something about grace and being saved.

These aren’t just cute dog stories (though we hope you find them engaging). They’re invitations to consider how the same God who designed dogs to be naturally loving and faithful wants to develop those same qualities in us.

We believe that our good God, who loves us deeply and is with us always, often uses the most ordinary things to teach us extraordinary truths. Sometimes that’s a sunset or a friendship or a moment of unexpected grace. And sometimes it’s a dog who wags his tail at 6:47 AM, reminding us that each day is a gift worth celebrating.

We’re honored to share these observations with you. Our prayer is that through these simple stories, you’ll catch a glimpse of the God who loves you unconditionally, who is always faithful, and who walks with you through every season of life.
His master replied, ‘Well done, good and faithful servant! You have been faithful with a few things; I will put you in charge of many things.’

Notice that the master doesn’t say, “Well done, good and spectacular servant!” or “Well done, good and impressive servant!” He commends faithfulness. In God’s economy, consistency matters more than flash, and reliability counts more than brilliance.

This is incredibly encouraging news for those of us who feel ordinary. We don’t have to cure cancer or become famous to live a significant life. We just have to be faithful with what God has given us: our relationships, our responsibilities, and our opportunities to serve.

We might not end up with any trophies or social media fame. But we’ll build something better: the kind of character that makes us truly valuable to the people who matter most. And we’ll discover that

35 there’s deep satisfaction in being someone others can depend on, just like Buddy.

After all, the world has plenty of flash-in-the-pan excitement. What it really needs is more people willing to love steadily, serve consistently, and stay faithful through all seasons. That’s a calling worthy of any life.

But when courage is called for, remember that we’re not alone. The same God who was with Joshua in the wilderness is with us in our high school hallways, family conversations, and friend groups. We can take the next right step, even when we’re scared, because He’s taking it with us.

Start small. Look for small opportunities to practice courage. Speak up in class. Try something new. Have an honest conversation. Each time we choose courage over comfort, we’re building spiritual muscle for more difficult times.

Hope also changes how we treat others. When we’re secure in God’s love and trust His plans for us, we can give a lot of encouragement, be patient with difficult people, and believe others can grow and change.

Let’s build this kind of hope. Keep track of God’s faithfulness in our lives. When we pray about something and see an answer, write it down. When God provides what we need, make a note. When He gives us strength for a challenge or peace during hard times, make sure to put it in writing. Over time, we will have our own collection of proof that God keeps His promises.

Max waits by the window every day because Katie has never failed to come home. As we experience God’s faithfulness in small things, we will find it easier to

60 trust Him with bigger things. Our hope will feel less like a maybe and more like something we can count on.

Take our position by the window of faith. Wait with confidence for God’s goodness. And remember: the same God who has been faithful to a lot of people for thousands of years is the same God who loves us and has good plans for our lives today. That’s worth waiting for with joy.

Final Thoughts

If you’ve made it this far, you’ve probably noticed something: dogs don’t read theology books or attend seminars on spiritual growth. They don’t have complicated ways for living well. They just know to do many of the things that make life meaningful. They’re excited, focused, loyal, generous, trusting, humble, faithful, rested, courageous, forgiving, serving, and hopeful.

But here’s what makes their example even more powerful: dogs live this way naturally because they know they’re loved and cared for by their families. Because they feel safe in that relationship they can be the best dogs they can be.

The world needs more people who live like good dogs: people who love without any conditions, serve without counting the cost, and trust in something bigger than themselves. People who can be disappointed without growing bitter, who can fail without losing hope, who can succeed without losing humility.

As you go through the ups and downs of these teenage years, remember that you’re not just figuring out what you want to do with your life. You’re discovering who God created you to be. And the same One who made dogs so naturally good at loving and living has even better plans, much better plans, for you.

So wag your tail at the gift of each new day. Stay close to the people who matter. Love generously. Trust deeply. Rest when you need to. Be brave when courage is called for. Forgive quickly. Serve joyfully. Hope confidently. In other words, live like you know you're loved. Because you are.

The good dogs have been trying to tell us this all along. Maybe it’s time we started listening.

Our despair is often based on incomplete information too. We can’t see the conversations happening on our behalf. We can’t witness the doors that are opening in places we’d never expect. We can’t perceive the ways God is working to create opportunities we can’t imagine.

Maria’s work for Ziggy was most intense during the period when he felt most hopeless. God’s work in our situation might be most active during the times when we feel most forgotten.

Every story of major failure and brokenness needs an ending. There needs to be a moment when we finally come home to the life that’s been waiting for us. Maybe it’s making up with family who won’t talk to us. Maybe it’s finding work that gives us purpose again. Maybe it’s the ultimate homecoming when we see Jesus face to face.

The question is this: will we keep believing that our “going home day” is real? Will we keep believing when the waiting stretches longer than we expected?

Our ultimate going-home day isn’t dependent on earthly circumstances. We might experience restoration in this life or the next. Relationships might heal quickly or only in eternity. Either way, our place in God’s family is secure.

Our home is being prepared. Someone loves us because of our whole story, including the broken parts. Keep our tail wagging. Keep our hope alive. Our going-home day is coming.

Final Thoughts

If you’ve made it to the end of these twelve lessons, thank you for staying. Thank you for listening. Thank you for walking a little further with us on this journey.

The love that wants the best for us can sometimes demand too much from us.

But it doesn’t have to be this way.

Families can honor their heritage while making space for new expressions of it. Parents can hold hopes for their children with open hands. Young people can respect where they come from while also embracing where they’re going. Love can exist without conditions. Worth can exist without performance.

We know because we’ve seen it. In the families who finally learned to breathe. In the young people who found their own voice without losing their family. In the parents who discovered their children’s different paths weren’t rejections but expansions of family legacy.

The dog’s father’s statue still stood at the training facility entrance. People still spoke respectfully about the legendary bloodline. The dog had honored his family, but not by following their exact path. He had honored them by serving where his actual gifts could help others. Sometimes the greatest calling is not doing what your family did, but discovering what you were made to do.

His paws walked a different path than his father’s. That did not make the path less valuable. It made it his own.

It’s learning that you can honor where you came from and still become who you’re meant to be. And somewhere along the way, you might find that the people who love you don’t actually need you to be perfect. They can love you even when you fall short of what they hoped for.

The dogs in those next stories will help us find it.

We hope you’ll join us there.

So whether you are the child of immigrants carrying the weight of their sacrifice, the heir to generations of family business, the one expected to follow religious footsteps, or simply someone who dreams differently than your family dreamed for you, we hope these stories have helped you breathe a little easier.

Your story can honor your family’s story without repeating it. Your path can respect your heritage while also expanding it. Your choices can show gratitude for where you came from while also embracing where you are going.

But to Sunny, it was pure treasure.

Amira found herself smiling as she watched Sunny’s pure enthusiasm. Here was a dog who could find complete joy in something so simple and worn out. He didn’t need a brand new toy or expensive equipment to be happy. That old tennis ball was enough to make his entire day.

Over the next few weeks, Amira continued to watch Sunny during her morning coffee time. Every day, he played with that same ball with the same level of excitement. It never got old for him. It never became boring or disappointing. Each time he saw it, his eyes lit up with genuine delight.

Amira began to think about her own attitude toward the church’s music ministry. She had been so focused on what they didn’t have that she had stopped appreciating what they did have. Yes, their equipment was older, but it still worked. Yes, their music stands were mismatched, but they held the music. Yes, the piano needed tuning, but it still made beautiful sounds when the congregation sang together.

More importantly, Amira realized she had been missing the real treasure in her work. Every Sunday, she got to help people worship God through music. She got to see elderly members singing hymns they had loved for decades. She got to hear children learning new songs about God’s love. She got to watch the whole congregation lift their voices together in praise.

These precious moments weren’t dependent on having perfect equipment or unlimited budgets. They were about hearts connecting with God through simple melodies and familiar words. The next Sunday, Amira approached the service with a different attitude. Instead of worrying about the microphone’s slight feedback or the piano’s imperfect tuning, she focused on the joy of making music together. When the adult choir sang with the kind of heartfelt emotion that only comes from years of faith, Amira felt tears in her eyes. When the children’s choir giggled their way through their special song, Amira couldn’t help but smile.

That week, Amira started a gratitude list for her music ministry. Instead of focusing on what was lacking, she wrote down what was working well. The list grew longer than she expected: dedicated choir members who showed up every week, a pianist who volunteered her time faithfully, a sound system that might be old but never failed them, and a congregation that sang with genuine hearts.

Amira learned something important from watching Sunny’s daily joy. Happiness doesn’t come from having the newest or best things. It comes from appreciating what you have and finding delight in simple blessings.

Just like Sunny could play with the same old tennis ball day after day with fresh enthusiasm, Amira discovered she could lead music ministry with renewed joy, even with the same limited resources.

The secret wasn’t in getting better equipment; it was in having a grateful heart that could see the treasure in what was already there.

From that point forward, whenever Amira felt discouraged about budget constraints or equipment limitations, she would think of Sunny’s pure delight with his worn-out tennis ball.
